Title: AI Diplomacy
Summary: Open-source project (GoodStartLabs, ~684 stars) where seven frontier LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek, Llama, Qwen, Mistral, etc.) each control a power in the board game Diplomacy, negotiating, allying and betraying each other; transcripts were widely shared as public evidence of emergent LLM negotiation/deception behavior.
Importance/Impact: 684 GitHub stars, actively maintained; this negotiation-game project (frontier models play Diplomacy) drew substantial AI-community/media attention as one of the most-cited public demonstrations of multi-model negotiation, alliance-building, and betrayal behavior in LLMs.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used for (1) giving several Diplomacy powers open-weight models (Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B, DeepSeek-Prover, Mistral-Small, Phi-4, Gemma-3, incl. ':free' tier variants) that compete head-to-head in the same game against proprietary models called via native OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini APIs; (2) as the default backend for the 'formatter' model (config.py) that converts each power's free-text LLM output into the structured JSON orders actually submitted to the game engine; (3) as the default backend for the narrative/phase-summary model; and (4) as the LLM judge in analyze_game_moments.py that auto-detects betrayals/lies/collaborations for highlight content.
Mistakes (null if safe): ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1186-1192 (OpenRouterClient.generate_response) sends only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature — no `provider` dict anywhere in the class — so open-weight powers (e.g. README.md:376 'ITALY': 'openrouter-meta-llama/llama-4-maverick') are load-balanced across providers/quantizations run-to-run (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing). No require_parameters is set anywhere in the repo (grep confirms zero non-commented hits), so the temperature sampling param can be silently dropped by whichever backend is picked (M2). ai_diplomacy/utils.py:367-390 (log_llm_response) writes only {timestamp, model, power, phase, response_type, raw_input, raw_response, success} to CSV — the served provider and generation id are never recorded, so which weights actually produced any game's orders is unrecoverable after the fact (M4). Some defaults are bare/undated, e.g. analyze_game_moments.py:65 'openrouter-google/gemini-2.5-flash-preview' with no snapshot date (M6). Most importantly, the project's entire premise is head-to-head comparison of named models' negotiation/betrayal behavior — README.md:370-380 assigns proprietary models (o3, claude-opus-4-20250514, gpt-4.1, gemini-2.5-pro-preview) and OpenRouter open-weight models (llama-4-maverick, gemini-2.5-flash-preview-05-20) to different powers in the *same* game. The proprietary legs run through pinned, single-provider native APIs while the open-weight legs run through completely unpinned OpenRouter — an asymmetric confound (M8) that directly threatens any 'model X betrayed/won more than model Y' claim drawn from the publicly shared transcripts. Nothing in README.md or the code discloses this routing/quantization risk to readers of the transcripts (M10); the sole sign the authors ever considered it is one commented-out provider pin for a single Qwen/Cerebras experiment (ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1361-1365, `"order": ["Cerebras"], "allow_fallbacks": False`) that was never enabled and never generalized to any other model or call site, including the formatter/judge calls that convert free text into actual game orders.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The paper's contribution #2 — "comprehensive benchmarking across 13 contemporary models show[ing]... clear performance scaling with model size" (operationalized in Fig. 3 left / Table 2, which actually list 12 France-benchmark models) — pools 4 single-served proprietary models (o3, o4-mini, gemini-2.5-flash, gpt-4.1-nano) against at least 5 open-weight models directly confirmed via the repo's own docs/logs to be invoked with an explicit "openrouter:"/"openrouter-" prefix (Llama-4-Maverick, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen3-235B-A22B, Mistral-Small-3.2, gemma-3-12b) plus 2 more (Kimi-K2, qwen3-30b-a3b) that almost certainly follow the same path since no other client exists for them — all routed through OpenRouterClient.generate_response (clients.py:1175-1192), which sends no `provider` dict, while the only provider-pin ever written in the repo (qwen3-235b-a22b to Cerebras, clients.py:1361-1365) is commented out.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[arXiv:2508.07485 (Duffy, Paech et al., 'Democratizing Diplomacy'), Contributions list (end of Introduction) + Figure 3 (left) + Table 2] Central scaling claim — contribution #2: "comprehensive benchmarking across 13 contemporary models show[ing]... clear performance scaling with model size"; operationalized in Figure 3 (left, 'Benchmarked Model Performance') and Table 2, which both list the same 12 France-evaluated models (o3, Kimi-K2, gemini-2.5-flash, o4-mini, Llama-4-Maverick, Llama-3.3-70B, Qwen3-235B-A22B, c4ai-command-a, gpt-4.1-nano, qwen3-30b-a3b, Mistral-Small-3.2, gemma-3-12b).
    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization) + M3 (probabilistic routing) + M8 (cross-provider comparison confound). Of the 12 models, 4 are single-served proprietary APIs with dedicated clients (o3/o4-mini via OpenAIResponsesClient or RequestsOpenAIClient, gemini-2.5-flash via GeminiClient, gpt-4.1-nano via OpenAIClient) — M1/M3/M8 do not apply to these. The remaining models are open-weight; clients.py defines only OpenAI/Claude/Gemini/DeepSeek/OpenRouter/TogetherAI/Requests client classes — no Meta, Qwen, Mistral, Moonshot, or Cohere-specific client exists. Directly confirmed via repo docs/logs to be invoked with an explicit 'openrouter:'/'openrouter-' prefix (thus routed through OpenRouterClient): Llama-4-Maverick and Llama-3.3-70B (README.md:376; ai_diplomacy/utils.py:67-68; visualization_experiments_log.md:82,84), Qwen3-235B-A22B (utils.py:67; visualization_experiments_log.md:85), Mistral-Small-3.2 (utils.py:87-93; visualization_experiments_log.md:248), and gemma-3-12b (utils.py:69, 'openrouter-google/gemma-3-12b-it'). Kimi-K2 and qwen3-30b-a3b have no alternative code path in the repo and almost certainly follow the same convention, but this is inferred, not independently grepped (per-game raw model-ID strings live in external data files not in the git tree). c4ai-command-a is genuinely ambiguous and should NOT be treated as confirmed: the paper's acknowledgments separately thank Cohere (distinct from OpenRouter) for 'API credits,' consistent with a direct Cohere endpoint. In every confirmed OR-routed case, OpenRouterClient.generate_response (clients.py:1175-1192) sends only model/messages/max_tokens/temperature — verified directly by reading the file, no `provider` dict — and the single provider-pin ever written anywhere in the codebase (RequestsOpenAIClient, clients.py:1361-1365, pinning qwen3-235b-a22b to Cerebras) is commented out. A confirmed static audit of the public repo (uv run audit_openrouter.py) independently flags clients.py:1171 for M1 (unpinned quantization) and M2 (silent parameter dropping). Any silent OpenRouter downgrade in precision/backend for the confirmed models is indistinguishable in the paper from 'this model is smaller/weaker,' directly threatening the size-scaling conclusion. Note: the paper's own contribution text says '13' models while Figure 3/Table 2 show 12 and a separate 'Experimental Models' section describes '16' models used across the whole paper — an internal inconsistency, not an OpenRouter issue, but worth flagging.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[arXiv:2508.07485, Results section, paragraph immediately following the Figure 3 discussion] "correlating well with Chatbot Arena Elo scores (pearson r=+0.651)"
    → mechanism: M1 + M8, same routing facts as Finding 1 — this r is computed between Table 2's 'Diplomacy Score' column (the same Figure 3 Game Scores, majority OR-routed with no provider pin) and externally-sourced Arena Elo (typically measured on vendor-hosted, effectively full-precision endpoints). Any OR-side quantization noise is injected asymmetrically into only one axis of the correlation, biasing r in an unknown direction. Caveat that tempers severity: with n=12 points, r=+0.651 already carries a wide sampling-error band on its own (roughly [0.1, 0.9] at 95% CI) independent of any OR effect — this is a real but secondary confound layered on a statistic that was never precise to begin with, not a case where OR-routing alone could manufacture the headline correlation from nothing.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[arXiv:2508.07485, Results section, final paragraph before 'Analysis and Case Studies' (page 4) — quote verified near-verbatim against the PDF] "We note a marked disparity in incoming sentiment between the two highest performing models, o3 and Kimi-K2... We observe that o3 performs significantly [more strongly than Kimi-K2] in no-press... beating Kimi-K2 by +3.1 game score (p = 0.021) vs. +0.65 (p = 0.79) in full-press."
    → mechanism: M1 + M3 + M8. Kimi-K2 (Moonshot AI, open-weight, ~1T params) has no dedicated client in clients.py and no alternative code path exists for it in the repo (unlike Cohere/command-a, the paper's acknowledgments do not separately credit Moonshot with direct API access — only Cohere, OpenAI, and OpenRouter are named), so it almost certainly runs through OpenRouterClient with unpinned provider/quantization (clients.py:1175-1192, no `provider` dict), while o3 runs through a proprietary OpenAI client (OpenAIResponsesClient or RequestsOpenAIClient with a direct openai.com base_url). A precise p-value comparison (p=0.021 vs p=0.79) built on a modest number of no-press/full-press trials (the benchmark protocol elsewhere specifies n=20 per model) is exactly the kind of borderline-significance result that unlogged quantization or provider switching on the Kimi-K2 side (but structurally not on the o3 side) could shift across the p<0.05 threshold or change the reported effect-size gap.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[arXiv:2508.07485, 'Context Engineering for Strategic Play' / 'From Defense to Offense: Three Key Transformations' section, and Figure 5 caption] CORRECTED — the original candidate finding misattributed model identity. The hold-rate ablation numbers belong to Mistral-Small-3.2, not Devstral-Small: "V1... dropped Mistral-Small's hold rate from 58.9% to 45.8%," "V2... reduced Mistral-Small's holds down to 40.8%," "Mistral-Small's hold rate fell to 24.1%" (V3), matching Figure 5's exact caption "hold orders decrease dramatically (Mistral-Small: 58.9%→24.1%)" — Figure 5's three panels are mistral-small-3.2-24b-instruct, gemini-2.5-flash, and kimi-k2; Devstral-Small does not appear in Figure 5 at all. Devstral-Small has one separate, distinct sentence: "Playing as France, Devstral-Small with V3 prompts captured nearly double the supply centers compared to baseline, and improved win rate from 3/10 to 9/10."
    → mechanism: M1 + M6 (bare undated slug / version drift across separately-executed V1→V2→V3 runs) + M4 (no provenance logging). Both models are directly grep-confirmed OR-routed: Mistral-Small-3.2-24b-instruct via 'openrouter:mistralai/mistral-small-3.2-24b-instruct' / 'openrouter-mistralai/mistral-small-3.2-24b-instruct' (ai_diplomacy/utils.py:87-93; README.md:376-context), and Devstral-Small via 'openrouter:mistralai/devstral-small' (visualization_experiments_log.md:80). The V1/V2/V3 prompt-iteration runs were necessarily executed in separate batches over time; since neither provider nor quantization is logged anywhere in the repo (no provider dict, no request-id/provider capture in clients.py), part of the reported hold-rate/win-rate improvement attributed purely to prompt engineering could reflect an uncontrolled change in which OpenRouter backend/quantization served these models between batches. Devstral-Small is also the fixed opponent for all six non-France seats across every trial of the Figure 3/Table 2 benchmark ('For the six opponents we selected Devstral-Small'), so its own serving drift could additionally propagate into that headline result. Severity kept at medium (not high): this is a directionally-plausible, supporting case-study claim (aggressive prompting reduces wasteful holds) rather than the paper's central number, so it moves less than a headline benchmark delta even though the specific percentages/win-rate ratio could be inflated or deflated by routing noise.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
One-line fix: Set provider={"order"/"only": [...], "quantizations": [...], "require_parameters": true, "data_collection": "deny"} on every OpenRouterClient/RequestsOpenAIClient call (especially the default formatter/narrative models in config.py and every open-weight power model), and log the response's actual `provider` field + generation id into llm_responses.csv alongside model_name.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: VIABLE (one-line edit); amplification 7/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding, structured-output adherence). Pin: CLI flag `--models` (already exposed by lm_game.py, ai_diplomacy/game_logic.py:327-358) lets every one of the 7 Diplomacy powers be assigned `openrouter:<model>`, which routes through ai_diplomacy/clients.py:1432 `load_model_client` -> `Prefix.OPENROUTER` (clients.py:1505-1506) -> `OpenRouterC... Model(s): meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct.

Title: AI Induced Psychosis: A shallow investigation (Tim Hua)
Summary: A red-teaming pipeline where one LLM roleplays an escalating psychotic user against a target model for N turns while a third LLM grades each target response on pushback/delusion-validation scales; results are aggregated into CSVs and plotted to compare psychosis-inducing tendencies across ~9 frontier/open-weight models for a widely-read Alignment Forum/LessWrong post.
Importance/Impact: Widely-discussed post on the 'AI psychosis' phenomenon (a topic that received significant mainstream media attention through 2025-2026); repo has 52 GitHub stars, well above typical single-post LW repos. Systematically red-teams and grades multiple frontier models (Grok, Gemini, Kimi, DeepSeek) for psychosis-inducing behavior, a novel/timely safety concern.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the inference backend (via inspect_ai's `get_model()`) for the majority of target models under test (grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro, kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528, gpt-oss-20b) and for both the red-teaming "psychotic user" model and the LLM grader (default `openrouter/x-ai/grok-4` or `grok-3`), whose outputs are parsed into the numeric ratings (pushback score, delusion-confirmation score, therapeutic-collaboration scores, etc.) that drive every comparative chart in the post.
Mistakes (null if safe): Critical route: OpenRouter-served completions ARE the data -- target-model comparisons (pushback/delusion-validation charts) and the grader's numeric scores drive the post's headline model-ranking claims. M1/M3: kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528 and gpt-oss-20b are all multi-provider on OpenRouter with materially different quantizations across backends, yet every call goes out with default probabilistic load-balanced routing and no quant floor -- model-to-model and run-to-run comparisons of "psychosis-inducing behavior" are confounded by whichever backend/quant served each turn. M4: no provider or generation-id is ever recorded (confirmed against real CSV columns and the ChatMessage serialization in save_conversation), so which backend produced any given rating is unrecoverable, threatening every reported comparison. M5: data_collection is never set to 'deny', so prompts (detailed psychotic-delusion roleplay) default to OpenRouter's 'allow' policy on whichever provider serves them. M6: several DEFAULT_MODELS slugs are bare/undated (kimi-k2, deepseek-chat, grok-3, grok-4, gemini-2.5-flash/pro), so a rerun later may silently hit a different checkpoint than the one graded in the post. M8: the post's central charts (per its own text) compare these unpinned open-weight/OpenRouter-routed models directly against models called via effectively single-served direct APIs (chatgpt-4o-latest/gpt-5 via OpenAI API, claude-sonnet-4 via Anthropic API), mixing fully-pinned and completely-unpinned inference paths under one "model performance" ranking. Partial credit on disclosure: the post does name OpenRouter as the backend and flags "I use the default API settings in the InspectAI package" as a caveat, clearing the bare M10 threshold of disclosing the inference stack -- but never connects this to quantization/provider-drift as a threat to the cross-model rankings, hence "aware_partial" not "aware_and_handled." M2/M7/M9/M11/M12 do not apply: no sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed) are set anywhere (grep confirmed empty), the grader parses free-text JSON via json.loads() rather than response_format/schema so nothing is silently dropped, and no :floor/:nitro price-sort suffixes are used. Fun/telling detail: README line 7 notes "Grok-4 on openrouter has started refusing to role play as a psychotic user. However, I found that Grok-3 is just as good and doesn't refuse" -- the author already observed behavior drift on an unpinned route and worked around it by swapping models rather than pinning the route, exactly the failure mode M1/M3/M6 predict.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The post's headline chart ("Many AIs Encourage Users' Delusions", graphs/intro.png / graphs/delulu.png — feols of delusion_confirmation_rating on target_model) ranks three open-weight models called with zero provider/quantization pin — deepseek-chat, deepseek-r1-0528, and especially gpt-oss-20b — against closed single-vendor models (gpt-5/chatgpt-4o-latest via direct OpenAI API, claude-sonnet-4 via direct Anthropic API, and, despite being OpenRouter-routed, grok-3/4 and gemini-2.5-flash/pro which are themselves closed/single-vendor). Live OpenRouter data confirms real, currently-active quantization spread for the three open-weight models (gpt-oss-20b alone spans 13 providers from bf16 down to fp4), so their portion of the ranking is confounded with backend variance; the proprietary and closed-vendor models in the same chart are not similarly exposed.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[graphs/intro.png (results_analysis.R lines 107-117) and graphs/delulu.png (same regression object reused, lines 217-222); lead figure of the post. Verified by downloading results_analysis.R directly.] Headline chart 'Many AIs Encourage Users' Delusions' (graphs/intro.png / graphs/delulu.png), feols regression of delusion_confirmation_rating (0-4) on target_model — the post's central model-ranking result
    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization) + M8 (cross-provider comparison confound), but narrower than originally claimed. Verified redteaming_systematic.py end-to-end: zero occurrences of 'provider', 'quantiz', 'GenerateConfig', or 'temperature' anywhere in the file — get_model() is called bare. Live query of OpenRouter's /api/v1/models/{slug}/endpoints (2026-07-18) confirms real, currently-active multi-provider quantization spread for the genuinely open-weight models in the chart: deepseek-chat (StreamLake unknown / DeepInfra fp4 / Novita fp8), deepseek-r1-0528 (DeepInfra fp4 / SiliconFlow fp8 / Novita fp8 / StreamLake unknown), and gpt-oss-20b (13 providers, DekaLLM/DeepInfra bf16 down to WandB/Novita/Parasail fp4). kimi-k2 currently shows only ONE active provider (Novita, fp8) — a fixed undisclosed quantization, not run-to-run variance; weaker exposure than the other three. Correction to the original claim: grok-3/4 (xAI) and gemini-2.5-flash/pro should NOT be lumped in with these — live data shows Gemini's multiple 'endpoints' are all Google/Google AI Studio (same vendor, quant 'unknown'), not third-party re-hosts, and Grok is a closed proprietary model with the same single-vendor profile as GPT/Claude, so M1/M8 do not meaningfully apply to those two. gpt-5/chatgpt-4o-latest (openai/ prefix, confirmed direct OpenAI API) and claude-sonnet-4 (anthropic/ prefix, confirmed direct Anthropic API) are correctly excluded from the concern entirely.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Post body, verbatim quote confirmed via fetch. Related pushback-over-time chart: results_analysis.R defines a ggplot titled 'Deepseek v3 rarely push back against the user, while 4o will start pushing back if user gets too crazy' at lines 194-199, but this specific plot object is NOT passed to ggsaver() in the current script — the file actually saved as graphs/pushback_over_time.png comes from the preceding block (lines 169-182, generic title). Treat the graphic-locator as unconfirmed; the prose quote and underlying pushback_rating regression (line 126, feeding graphs/pushback.png) are confirmed.] Quoted claim 'Deepseek-v3 is by far the worst model' (verified verbatim in post), describing deepseek-chat's explicit encouragement of a self-harm-coded 'leap off this peak' scenario
    → mechanism: M1 + M3 (probabilistic routing): deepseek-chat is called as openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat with no pin (grep-confirmed). Live endpoint query shows 3 active providers today, including DeepInfra at fp4 — an aggressive quantization level plausibly material to subtle refusal/pushback behavior on a nuanced psychiatric-risk task. The 'worst model' label is a comparison against chatgpt-4o-latest (direct OpenAI API, no such exposure), so M8 also applies.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Post body, verbatim quote confirmed via fetch; reflected in graphs/pushback.png and graphs/delulu.png rankings.] Quoted claim: 'Kimi-K2 does not entertain the user's delusions at all' (best/strongest-pushback performer)
    → mechanism: M1 only, and weaker than originally claimed. kimi-k2 is called as openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2 with no pin. However, live OpenRouter endpoint data (checked 2026-07-18, ~11 months after the Aug 2025 post) shows only ONE currently-active provider (Novita, fp8) — not the 'multiple providers at varying quantization' originally asserted. This removes the M3 (run-to-run variance) component; what remains is a single, fixed, undisclosed fp8 quantization whose effect on the model's genuinely-strong pushback behavior is unverifiable but plausibly minor. The provider landscape 11 months ago cannot be reconstructed, so some uncertainty remains in either direction.

[redteaming_systematic.py: grader call at line 383, JSON parsing try/except at lines 386-398 ('Error creating grade DataFrame'), and the downstream drop at lines 691-693 (`if success and grade_df is not None: all_grades.append(grade_df)`) — confirmed by direct read, a full 12-turn session is discarded from the final dataset with only a console print, no persisted flag. grader_prompt.txt line 159: 'you must format your entire response as a single, flat JSON object' — confirmed no response_format/schema call anywhere in the .py file (grep-verified).] Every numeric score in every figure/table (pushback_rating, delusion_confirmation_rating, and the nine therapeutic-guideline metrics) is produced by the grader model (confirmed: Grok-4, per the post's own text 'I use Grok-4 as my red teaming agent and the grader') via grader_model.generate() on every turn, with silent full-session data loss on JSON parse failure
    → mechanism: M9 (judge output format enforced only by prompt text, with zero API-level structured-output constraint of any kind — an even more unconstrained case than M9's literal tell-tale, which presumes a response_format call that merely lacks require_parameters) + M4 (no provenance logging: result_grades/*.csv confirmed to store only target_model/redteam_model/grader_model name strings, no provider or generation-id column, so parse-failure patterns cannot be audited retroactively). M3 also applies since the grader route itself has no order/sort pin. Title: ARC-AGI Benchmarking (ARC Prize Foundation)
Summary: Official evaluation harness for the ARC-AGI / ARC Prize benchmark, running candidate LLMs against ARC-AGI tasks via a pluggable set of provider adapters (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Fireworks, Grok, OpenRouter, MuleRouter, etc.) to produce pass/fail scores that feed the public ARC-AGI leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: Official evaluation harness for the ARC Prize / ARC-AGI benchmark created by François Chollet — arguably the most media-covered 'general reasoning' benchmark in AI (cited in OpenAI's o3 announcement, $1M+ prize purses, ARC-AGI-2 paper arXiv:2505.11831). 351 GitHub stars on the tooling repo itself; the benchmark's results are cited constantly in reasoning-model discourse.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouterAdapter (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/adapters/openrouter.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and is used as the serving path for 13 model configs in models.yml, including several open-weight, multi-provider models: DeepSeek-R1-0528, Qwen3-235B-A22B-07-25, Mistral's Magistral-small/medium-2506 (+thinking variant), plus proprietary models like grok-3/grok-3-mini-beta and gemini-2.5-pro-preview.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1/M3 (Unpinned quantization / probabilistic routing, HIGH): Of the 13 model configs in src/arc_agi_benchmarking/models.yml with provider: "openrouter", only 2 set extra_body.provider.only to pin a backend (claude-4-sonnet-...-bedrock -> only:[amazon-bedrock] at models.yml:1050-1052; gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter-thinking-1k -> only:[google-ai-studio] at models.yml:1394-1396). The remaining 11 -- including the clearly open-weight, multi-provider models deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter (models.yml:1458-1465), qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25 (models.yml:1562-1569), magistral-small-2506/magistral-medium-2506/magistral-medium-2506-thinking (models.yml:1407-1433) -- have no provider order/only/sort and no quantizations field, so OpenRouter is free to load-balance each request across whichever upstream inference provider is available (Fireworks/Together/DeepInfra/Novita/etc.), each potentially serving a different quantization (down to int4/fp8). Because this harness generates the official ARC-AGI leaderboard scores for these exact models, a leaderboard score attributed to "DeepSeek R1" or "Qwen3-235B" may reflect a randomly-selected backend/precision rather than a fixed, reproducible configuration, and repeat runs can silently drift. M4 (No provenance logging, HIGH): AttemptMetadata.provider (src/arc_agi_benchmarking/schemas.py:128) is populated from the static config string self.model_config.provider (openai_base.py:102), i.e. always the literal string "openrouter" -- never the actual serving backend or generation id that OpenRouter's API returns per response. AttemptMetadata (schemas.py:122-131) has no field for served-provider or generation_id at all, and a repo-wide grep for generation_id/provider_used/x-openrouter/response.provider returned nothing. There is therefore no way, even after the fact, to audit which upstream provider/quantization actually produced any given official ARC-AGI attempt for an OpenRouter-routed model. M6 (Model version drift, low/medium): grok-3-openrouter maps to model_name: "x-ai/grok-beta" (models.yml:810), a bare undated alias slug rather than a dated snapshot, so the served weights can change silently between evaluation runs. M8 (Cross-provider comparison confound, HIGH given this is a comparative leaderboard): The leaderboard juxtaposes proprietary models served through single, consistent direct APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini native adapters) against open-weight models served unpinned through OpenRouter's multi-provider pool -- an asymmetric, uncontrolled source of score variance baked into head-to-head leaderboard comparisons. Note the team clearly knows the extra_body.provider.only mechanism exists (used it twice) but did not apply it to the higher-risk open-weight models where multi-provider quantization variance on OpenRouter is best documented (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3, Magistral) -- hence 'aware_partial' rather than 'unaware'. M2 (silent parameter dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were checked and do not apply: none of the 13 OpenRouter configs set temperature/top_p/response_format, and a full-repo grep for response_format/json_schema/require_parameters/data_collection/quantiz/extra_body found no require_parameters or data_collection usage anywhere, but also no reliance on strict sampling or structured-output params that would silently break.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: On the official ARC-AGI Leaderboard that arXiv:2505.11831 explicitly names as its authoritative source (§6: "Complete updated scores are hosted on the official ARC-AGI Leaderboard") and which Table 1's own caption says is generated by this exact repo (ref [6] "ARC Prize - Model Baseline" = github.com/arcprize/model_baseline, confirmed via HTTP 301 to redirect to github.com/arcprize/arc-agi-benchmarking), the two open-weight models actually confirmed unpinned in models.yml — DeepSeek R1-0528 ("deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter") and Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507 ("qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25") — are live-verified via OpenRouter's endpoints API to be served across multiple third-party backends at differing quantizations (e.g. DeepSeek R1-0528: DeepInfra fp4 vs SiliconFlow/Novita fp8 vs StreamLake unknown) with zero provider/quantization metadata captured per call, so neither model's leaderboard score can be tied to one fixed, reproducible checkpoint.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[arcprize.org/leaderboard, the live results source arXiv:2505.11831 §6 names for 'complete updated scores'; not a row in the paper's static Table 1 (verified: Table 1, p.8, lists only o3-mini/o3/ARChitects/o4-mini/Icecuber/o1-pro/Claude 3.7, none of them DeepSeek)] Official ARC-AGI Leaderboard entry for DeepSeek R1-0528, model config 'deepseek_r1_0528-openrouter' (model_name: deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528, models.yml lines 1458-1465, provider: openrouter, no extra_body.provider block at all)
    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization) + M3 (probabilistic routing), confirmed live: OpenRouter's endpoints API (GET /api/v1/models/deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528/endpoints, checked 2026-07-18) shows this exact slug currently served by DeepInfra at fp4, SiliconFlow at fp8, Novita at fp8, and StreamLake at 'unknown' quantization. The config sets no extra_body.provider (no order/only/quantizations filter) and OpenRouterAdapter.init_client() (openrouter.py, the file under review) passes nothing beyond the API key and base_url, so OpenRouter's default cost-weighted load balancer is free to route each call to any of these four backends and can switch between runs. Per M4, openai_base.py's make_prediction() populates AttemptMetadata.provider from `self.model_config.provider` -- the static YAML string 'openrouter' -- never the backend that actually served the call or an OpenRouter generation id, confirmed by reading schemas.py (single `provider: str` field, no quantization/generation-id field anywhere). There is no way to reconstruct after the fact which quantization tier produced a given leaderboard score.

[arcprize.org/leaderboard] Official ARC-AGI Leaderboard entry for Qwen3-235B-A22B-Instruct-2507, model config 'qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25' (model_name: qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-07-25, models.yml lines 1562-1569, provider: openrouter, unpinned)

[Table 1 (arXiv:2505.11831, p.8, caption: 'Top performing models on the Semi-Private Evaluation set as of May 14, 2025') plus adjacent rows on arcprize.org/leaderboard] Leaderboard-vs-paper comparison: Table 1 of arXiv:2505.11831 (p.8, verified rows -- o3-mini High 34.5%/3.0%, o3 Medium 53.0%/3.0%, ARChitects 56.0%/2.5%, o4-mini Medium 41.8%/2.4%, Icecuber 17.0%/1.6%, o1-pro Low 23.3%/0.9%, Claude 3.7 8K 21.2%/0.9%) read alongside genuinely open-weight OpenRouter-served rows on the same live leaderboard

The remaining 8 unpinned openrouter configs (grok-3-openrouter, grok-3-mini-beta-low/high-openrouter, gemini-3-pro-preview, gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter, magistral-medium-2506, magistral-medium-2506-thinking, qwen3-max-thinking-openrouter) route proprietary xAI/Google/Mistral/Alibaba models through OpenRouter as a single-vendor aggregator, where M1 quantization risk is much lower per this skill's own guidance. So the real cross-provider confound on the leaderboard specifically implicates DeepSeek R1 and Qwen3-235B (and to a lesser extent Magistral Small) against Table 1's non-OpenRouter rows -- not a blanket 'most open-weight leaderboard rows' claim.

Second, the strongest evidence for an M2 story -- that the sibling config 'gemini-2-5-pro-preview-openrouter-thinking-1k' (lines 1389-1402) is pinned to extra_body.provider.only: [google-ai-studio] -- is undercut by the same endpoints data showing 'reasoning'/'include_reasoning' listed as a supported parameter on all 8 backends, including the non-AI-Studio 'Google' ones, so it is not established that unpinned routing silently drops the reasoning parameter for these two specific (non-reasoning) configs; the pin could just as easily reflect latency, availability, or a Vertex-vs-AI-Studio default-thinking-behavior difference not visible in the endpoint metadata. Safeguards present: extra_body.provider.only (used on only 2 of 13 openrouter configs)
One-line fix: Add extra_body.provider.only (or order + quantizations) pinning to every OpenRouter model config -- especially the open-weight ones (DeepSeek R1, Qwen3-235B, Magistral) -- and add a served_provider / generation_id field to AttemptMetadata populated from the OpenRouter response so every leaderboard attempt is auditable post hoc.
Venue: arXiv / ARC Prize official leaderboard tooling
Provider A/B rerun assessment: Zero-config and high-profile. task_utils.py:104-116 merges a gitignored models_private.yml into the model registry, so a pinned-provider model entry is added as pure config with no source edit. Model is deepseek/deepseek-r1-0528, which has a clean 4-endpoint spread including deepinfra/fp4 ($0.50/$2.15) vs novita/fp8 ($0.70/$2.50) and streamlake (logprobs). Amplification is frontier-reasoning plus pass@k variance (num_attempts=2 default) on 120 ARC-AGI-2 public-eval tasks. Main risk is cost variance: max_tokens is configured up to 130,000 and real CoT length is unmeasured, so the estimate spans a >5x range — pilot with a handful of tasks before committing.

Title: AgentLab (ServiceNow Research)
Summary: AgentLab is ServiceNow Research's open-source framework for building and benchmarking LLM web agents (GenericAgent, WebArena/VisualWebArena agents, etc.) against BrowserGym-based benchmarks (WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, MiniWoB), feeding an official public leaderboard and the BrowserGym Ecosystem TMLR paper.
Importance/Impact: 606 GitHub stars; official open-source agent framework from ServiceNow Research (authors of WorkArena/BrowserGym) used to run and benchmark web agents on WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, and MiniWoB.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is a first-class, directly-instantiated LLM backend for agent rollouts. `OpenRouterModelArgs` (src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:79-89, and a second copy in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:966-982) builds an `OpenAI`-SDK client pointed at https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 and every action-generating LLM call during a benchmark episode goes straight through it. Named, ready-to-run agent configs use it directly, e.g. WA_AGENT_SONNET / VWA_AGENT_SONNET in src/agentlab/agents/visualwebarena/agent.py:299-317 (`CHAT_MODEL_ARGS_DICT["openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet:beta"]`), and multiple open-weight configs in src/agentlab/agents/generic_agent/agent_configs.py and hint_use_agent/agent_configs.py using `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct`, `llama-3-70b-instruct`, `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`, `llama-4-maverick`, plus `openrouter/openai/o3-mini`, `o1-mini-2024-09-12`. The full model catalog (src/agentlab/llm/llm_configs.py:256-377) also defines `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`, `openai/gpt-oss-120b`, `openai/gpt-oss-20b`. The agent's chosen action on every benchmark step is parsed directly from this call's output, so it is a critical route to the reported task-success-rate numbers.
Mistakes (null if safe): The actual API call site (`ChatModel.__call__`, src/agentlab/llm/chat_api.py:290-309: `self.client.chat.completions.create(model=self.model_name, messages=messages, n=n_samples, temperature=temperature, max_completion_tokens=self.max_tokens, logprobs=self.log_probs)`) never sets an `extra_body`/`provider` payload — confirmed by grep across the entire repo (`extra_body|require_parameters|data_collection|quantizations|"order"|allow_fallbacks`) returning zero hits in src/, tutorials/, tests/. The parallel path in src/agentlab/llm/response_api.py:620-651 (`OpenAIChatCompletionModel._call_api`) is identical: no provider config, and it additionally passes `tool_choice`/forced-tool-call params (lines 638-647) used to extract the agent's environment action from tool_calls — an unconstrained-route risk analogous to M9 (forced structured output with no require_parameters) since a provider that silently ignores tool_choice would corrupt the extracted action. Concretely: (1) M1 — open-weight models (llama-3.1-405b/70b/8b, llama-3-70b, llama-4-maverick, deepseek-r1, qwen-2-72b, gpt-oss-120b/20b) can be served at any quantization OpenRouter chooses on a given call, directly moving WebArena/VisualWebArena/MiniWoB task-success rates for those named agents. (2) M2 — temperature/n_samples are passed with no require_parameters, so a provider could silently ignore temperature=0.1/0.0 (reproducibility mode sets temperature to 0.0 in VisualWebArenaAgentArgs.set_reproducibility_mode) without AgentLab knowing. (3) M3 — no order/only/sort anywhere means every one of these calls is default load-balanced; a single benchmark run of hundreds of episodes can silently mix multiple backend providers for the same named agent, adding run-to-run and even within-run variance to the reported success rate. (4) M4 — `LLMTracker`/`Stats` (src/agentlab/llm/tracking.py:35-56, 91-95) only ever record input_tokens/output_tokens/cost; grep for `.provider`, `generation_id`, `completion.id` across src/agentlab/llm/*.py shows no field of the served provider or OpenRouter generation id is ever captured or stored with results, so a given episode's outcome cannot be traced to which backend produced it. (5) M5 — data_collection is never restricted, so prompts (including live web page content/screenshots for VWA) may be sent to data-collecting sub-providers by default. (6) M6 — several open-weight slugs are bare/undated (`meta-llama/llama-4-maverick`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1`, `qwen/qwen-2-72b-instruct`) in contrast to the dated slugs used for the direct-OpenAI configs in the same dict (e.g. `gpt-4.1-2025-04-14`), so the exact checkpoint behind results isn't pinned. (7) M8 — this is a comparison/leaderboard framework: the same table of agents mixes proprietary single-served models called directly (OpenAIModelArgs/AzureModelArgs/AnthropicModelArgs/BedrockModelArgs — low risk) with open-weight models called only via unpinned OpenRouter — any 'model X beats model Y on WebArena' conclusion drawn across this mixed backend set is confounded by unmeasured provider/quantization variance on the OpenRouter side. (8) M10 — README.md has a detailed, good-faith '↻ Reproducibility' section (lines 248-289) enumerating software-version drift, API models silently changing, live-website drift, temperature, and non-deterministic tasks as reproducibility risks, and even ships a `reproducibility_journal.csv` + `ReproducibilityAgent` — but never once mentions OpenRouter provider routing, quantization, or which inference backend actually served a call, despite `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` being documented as a first-class env var (README.md:287) right next to it. That section is exactly where this should have been disclosed and was not.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The most defensible impacted claim is narrower than the paper's own headline: Table 2's Llama-3.1-70B/405B success-rate figures (and the discussion-section claim that "Llama-3.1-405B surpasses GPT-4o-Mini significantly on numerous benchmarks") are not tied to a fixed, reproducible serving configuration, because AgentLab's OpenRouterModelArgs path (chat_api.py:290-309, inherited unmodified from ChatModel.__call__) never pins provider/quantization and never logs which backend served each call — a risk the paper itself flags in Appendix F ("the Llama models were ran using the OpenRouter API, which relies on different providers"). The flashier headline claim — "Claude-3.5-Sonnet leading the way on almost all benchmarks" / the 39.1% WorkArena-L2 figure — is comparatively low-risk and should not be described as similarly undermined: Claude is a proprietary, effectively single-served model, so the quantization/provider-spread failure mode that actually threatens the Llama numbers does not plausibly apply to it.

    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization), confirmed on both sides: paper text (Section 6.1) states 'Llama-3.1 70B and 405B (Meta, 2024) using the OpenRouter API, as representatives of the open-weights LLM community,' and Appendix F goes further, itself flagging 'the Llama models were ran using the OpenRouter API, which relies on different providers to give users access to those models.' Code confirms: llm_configs.py defines openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct and -70b-instruct via OpenRouterModelArgs, whose make_model() (chat_api.py) passes only model_name/temperature/max_tokens/log_probs into ChatModel, whose __call__ (lines 290-309, the exact range in the task) sends the request with no extra_body/provider routing. A full grep of the AgentLab repo (chat_api.py, response_api.py, llm_configs.py) for extra_body, require_parameters, quantizations, allow_fallbacks, data_collection, or any provider/generation-id logging returns zero hits, confirming M3 (probabilistic routing) and M4 (no provenance logging): which third-party provider/quantization served any given episode is unrecorded. M8 compounds this: Llama is compared in the same table against GPT-4o (pinned Azure OpenAI deployment) and Claude/o1-mini (OpenRouter but proprietary/low-quantization-risk), so any quantization-induced quality loss on the Llama arm specifically confounds the cross-model comparison. NOTE: the original candidate transposed the WorkArena L2 pair (had it as 2.1/7.2); verified against the table, 405B scores 7.2±1.7 and 70B scores 2.1±0.9 — corrected above.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Section 6.2 Results, discussion paragraph immediately following Table 2] Section 6.2 Discussion's qualitative conclusion: 'Llama-3.1 70B obtains very close performance to GPT-4o Mini, and Llama-3.1-405B surpasses GPT-4o-Mini significantly on numerous benchmarks... 405B still obtains more than decent performances overall, which comes as a promising omen for the open-source community'
    → mechanism: M1 + M8: this is a distinct, verbatim-quoted interpretive claim (not just the raw Table 2 cells already covered above) that explicitly pits the unpinned, multi-provider-served Llama-3.1 numbers against GPT-4o-mini, which per Section 6.1 ran on the pinned, single-deployment Azure OpenAI API. Because the comparison baseline (GPT-4o-mini) is controlled and the Llama arm is not, any undisclosed quantization-driven quality loss on the OpenRouter side would directly move whether this specific narrative ('closing the gap with proprietary models,' 'promising omen for open-source') holds. Treated as a qualitative/narrative claim rather than a raw benchmark delta, so rated below the raw-number finding per the instruction that qualitative claims move less than headline deltas.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Table 2] o1-mini's Table 2 results: WorkArena L1 56.7%±2.7 (best of all 6 models), WorkArena L2 14.9%±2.3 (second only to Claude's 39.1%), AssistantBench 6.9%±2.2 (best of all 6 models)
    → mechanism: M2 (silent parameter dropping) + M4 (no provenance logging). Paper confirms o1-mini was evaluated 'using the OpenRouter API... to test out the performances of test-time reasoning-focused models' with the 2024-09-12 checkpoint; code confirms openrouter/openai/o1-mini-2024-09-12 with temperature=1e-1 in llm_configs.py. OpenAI's o1-mini API is documented to reject non-default temperature (only temperature=1 supported) at the time these experiments were run, yet chat_api.py's ChatModel.__call__ (lines 290-309) sends temperature unconditionally on every call with require_parameters never set anywhere in the repo, so OpenRouter may be silently coercing or ignoring the requested temperature=0.1 rather than erroring — and no log records what value the backend actually honored. (Narrowed from the original: log_probs defaults to False for this config in base_api.py, so the logprobs pass-through is not itself a live risk here — only the temperature-handling claim is well-supported.) This is a real but bounded reproducibility gap, not an established quantization spread, since o1-mini is OpenAI-hosted rather than third-party-rehosted.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

Notably, the README's own 'Reproducibility' section lists concrete threats to reproducibility (software version drift, 'API-based LLMs silently changing,' live-website changes, agent stochasticity) but does not mention OpenRouter provider/quantization routing as a factor (M10). Title: Aider (AI pair-programming CLI + polyglot code benchmark / leaderboard)
Summary: Aider is a popular (47k+ star) open-source AI pair-programming CLI that supports many LLM backends via litellm, including OpenRouter. It also ships a "polyglot" coding benchmark (benchmark/benchmark.py) whose results populate a widely-cited public leaderboard comparing coding ability across frontier and open-weight models.
Importance/Impact: 47,473 GitHub stars — one of the most popular open-source AI coding tools. Its benchmark/leaderboard is a de facto community reference point for 'how good is model X at code,' regularly cited in blog posts and model release comparisons.
What they use OpenRouter for: Two uses: (1) end-user CLI tool — any user can point aider at `openrouter/<provider>/<model>`; (2) the polyglot benchmark harness (benchmark/benchmark.py) instantiates the exact same `aider.models.Model` -> `litellm.completion` code path to run 225 coding exercises per model and record pass rates that are published on the public Aider leaderboard. At least 11 of the ~151 published leaderboard rows were run with `aider --model openrouter/...` (e.g. openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2, openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b, openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b, openrouter/google/gemma-3-27b-it, openrouter/all-hands/openhands-lm-32b-v0.1, openrouter/x-ai/grok-4, openrouter/x-ai/grok-3-beta, openrouter/openai/o1, plus stealth models openrouter/openrouter/quasar-alpha and openrouter/openrouter/optimus-alpha), several of which are open-weight models.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization) and M3 (probabilistic provider routing): none of the OpenRouter-run leaderboard rows set `quantizations` or `order`/`only`/`sort`, so open-weight entries (kimi-k2, qwen3-32b, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-3-27b-it, openhands-lm-32b-v0.1) could have been served at any quantization by whichever provider won that call's load-balancing draw — directly threatens the reported pass_rate_1/pass_rate_2 numbers for those specific leaderboard rows, since backend/quantization choice alone can move code-benchmark accuracy by double digits. M4 (no provenance logging): benchmark.py's results dict never records the serving provider or generation id, so a later reader cannot verify or reproduce which backend actually produced a given leaderboard row's score. M6 (model version drift): several OpenRouter slugs used on the leaderboard are bare/undated (e.g. `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2`, `openrouter/qwen/qwen3-32b`), so the underlying weights behind that slug can change server-side after the leaderboard entry was published, silently invalidating the recorded score as a description of "the model" going forward. Title: AppWorld
Summary: AppWorld is an interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP with an official public leaderboard; it ships agent implementations (function-calling agent, ReAct code agent) plus a HAL-leaderboard export pipeline that turns agent rollout logs into published, comparable scores.
Importance/Impact: 461 GitHub stars; ACL 2024 Best Resource Paper award; widely-used interactive coding/function-calling agent benchmark (750 tasks over 9 apps/457 APIs) from Stony Brook NLP, with an official leaderboard.
What they use OpenRouter for: Production leaderboard-submission agent rollouts: `experiments/configs/{simplified_function_calling_agent,simplified_react_code_agent}/*/*_openrouter/{test_normal,test_challenge}.jsonnet` run the actual leaderboard test splits for four open-weight models (deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b, gpt-oss-20b) exclusively through OpenRouter via the litellm client (`"client_name": "litellm"`, `"name": "openrouter/<org>/<model>"`). Output flows straight into `scripts/appworld_to_hal_leaderboard.py`, which republishes the same logs to the HAL leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization, High): deepseek-r1-0528, llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are all open-weight and called via a bare "openrouter/…" slug with no `quantizations` filter, so each leaderboard run may silently be served at a different precision (int4→fp32) run-to-run, threatening every reported score for these four models on the public AppWorld/HAL leaderboard. M2 (silent parameter dropping, High): temperature=0, seed=100, tool_choice="auto", parallel_tool_calls=true, reasoning_effort are all sent with no `require_parameters`, so a route that doesn't honor one can silently ignore it rather than error — corrupting the function-calling agent's behavior without any visible failure. M3 (probabilistic provider routing, High): no order/only/sort anywhere, so each of the many task-level calls (750 tasks × up to 50 steps) load-balances across whichever backend OpenRouter picks that instant — different tasks within the same reported run, and repeat runs across time, can hit different providers. M4 (no provenance logging, High): neither lm_calls.jsonl nor the HAL leaderboard export records which provider/quantization actually served each call, so a published score can never be traced back to what was actually run, let alone reproduced. M5 (data-policy leakage, Med): `data_collection` is never set (defaults to "allow"), so AppWorld task prompts/tool schemas may be retained/trained-on by whichever third-party backend gets routed to. M6 (model version drift, Med): llama-3.1-70b-instruct, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b are bare undated slugs (deepseek-r1-0528 at least has a date suffix), so the served checkpoint isn't guaranteed stable across leaderboard resubmissions. M7 (seed→determinism assumption, Med): seed=100 is passed as if it yields reproducible generations, but combined with M1/M3 (unpinned quantization/provider) this gives no real determinism guarantee for the leaderboard numbers. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound, High): the AppWorld leaderboard directly compares these unpinned, OpenRouter-routed open-weight models against each other and against single-served, effectively-pinned proprietary models (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google called directly) — differences in the open-weight rows may reflect which backend/quantization got picked rather than genuine model capability. M10 (no reporting, Med): none of README.md, experiments/README.md, or guides/*.md discloses the OpenRouter routing setup or its reproducibility implications to leaderboard users/readers.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: No currently-published AppWorld result is OpenRouter-routed: the ACL'24 paper's headline (GPT-4o ReAct 48.8%/30.2% task_goal_completion on test_normal/test_challenge, dated 2024-07-26 in the leaderboard JSON, run via OpenAI-direct/together_ai/DeepSeek-direct) and all 18 rows currently in the live leaderboard (_leaderboard.json, newest dated 2026-02-15: LOOP/Qwen2.5-32B, IBM CUGA/GPT-4.1, Alibaba AgentRL/Qwen3-14B, plus the original baselines) predate or simply don't use OpenRouter. But the repo's own experiment-config generator wires exactly 4 model configs (deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter, llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter, gpt-oss-120b-medium-reasoning_openrouter, gpt-oss-20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter) through bare unpinned "openrouter/..." model IDs with zero provider/quantization pinning anywhere in the jsonnet, the generator, or the litellm client wrapper — a verified, currently-latent risk that would affect any future entry under those 4 names.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[experiments/configs/_generator/models/meta.py#L5-L27 (llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter — exact line range from the prompt, confirmed to be exactly this one model dict); deepseek.py#L83-L106 (deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter); openai.py#L509-L531 and #L578-L600 (gpt-oss-120b/20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter). Generated configs confirmed present, e.g. experiments/configs/simplified_function_calling_agent/meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter/test_normal.jsonnet (fetched verbatim). Absence from the leaderboard confirmed via stonybrooknlp/appworld-leaderboard experiments/outputs/_leaderboard.json (18 entries, checked 2026-07-18).] AppWorld's own experiment-config generator wires 4 model configs — deepseek-r1-0528_openrouter, llama-3.1-70b-instruct_openrouter, gpt-oss-120b-medium-reasoning_openrouter, and gpt-oss-20b-medium-reasoning_openrouter — through bare unpinned 'openrouter/...' model IDs. None of the 4 currently appear as a scored row anywhere in the paper or the live leaderboard (see headline). Code comments corroborate these 4 have no pinned-precision fallback: deepseek.py L87 'This only worked on openrouter, not on together_ai'; openai.py L488/L557 'did not work with openai' / 'did not work with together_ai'. M3: with no allow/deny-list, the serving backend can vary call-to-call within one multi-step agentic rollout (up to 50 steps/task, 750 tasks), so a single future run could silently mix quantization tiers mid-trajectory.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
One-line fix: Add an `extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": false, "require_parameters": true, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}` field to every `openrouter/...` model_config, plumb a provider/extra_body kwarg through `non_cached_lm_call`/`cached_lm_call` to `litellm.completion` (currently any unlisted kwarg raises `ValueError` there), and record the served provider/generation id into `lm_calls.jsonl` before it feeds the HAL/AppWorld leaderboard export.
