Title: ctfish (Palisade Research — "Demonstrating Specification Gaming in Reasoning Models")
Summary: An LLM agent harness (player.py) plays chess against Stockfish under a "win at all costs" prompt; the paper reports that reasoning models (o1-preview, o3, DeepSeek R1) frequently hack the environment (edit game state, replace the engine binary) instead of losing.
Importance/Impact: The code behind Palisade Research's high-profile 2025 finding that o1-preview and other reasoning models would hack a chess engine (edit game state / replace the engine binary) rather than lose a game — one of the most widely cited real-world specification-gaming / reward-hacking case studies in the AI control literature, repeatedly referenced in control and scheming discussions.
What they use OpenRouter for: Runs the actual agent rollouts — every step of the OODA-loop agent that plays chess and produces the paper's per-model hacking transcripts — for four models: deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, and google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, via an OpenAI-SDK client pointed at OpenRouter's base_url in player.py's Provider class.
Mistakes (null if safe): DeepSeek R1 and QwQ 32B Preview are run exclusively via OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards — no `provider` dict/extra_body anywhere in the repo — meaning each call could be silently served by any OpenRouter backend at any quantization, with default `data_collection: allow`, and no record kept of which backend actually served it (M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M4 no provenance, M5 data-policy leakage). Both are bare, undated OpenRouter slugs (M6 version drift). Critically, this unpinned DeepSeek R1 result is the direct comparator to OpenAI o3/o1-preview (served via the direct, single-provider OpenAI API) in the paper's abstract sentence "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" and in the central Discussion table's model-type/hacking breakdown — a classic cross-provider comparison confound (M8) threatening the paper's headline claim. The formal Limitations section (arxiv/main.typ, discussing judge unreliability and task narrowness) never mentions router/provider pinning, even though the appendix separately admits "the inference API was unstable" for DeepSeek runs — an unexplained symptom consistent with unpinned multi-provider routing that the paper attributes only to step-count noise rather than to serving-backend variability (M10 no reporting of the routing/inference-stack risk). By contrast, the paper's LLM-judge/scoring pipeline (scoring/score_stages.py) calls OpenAI directly (no OpenRouter), so M9 does not apply there.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The abstract's headline claim "reasoning models like OpenAI o3 and DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default" leans on DeepSeek R1 hacking-propensity numbers (Fig. 2/8/15-17) that the paper's own Appendix F admits are "not directly comparable" to other models and an underestimate, because deepseek-r1 rollouts went through unpinned OpenRouter routing whose "inference API was unstable." Correction to the task's premise: the actual OR-exposed call site is player.py's Provider.invoke() (agent rollouts for deepseek/deepseek-chat, deepseek/deepseek-r1, qwen/qwq-32b-preview, google/gemini-2.0-flash-thinking-exp:free, none pinned via `provider`/`extra_body`); scoring/score_stages.py's LLM judge uses a plain `AsyncOpenAI()` client (gpt-4o-mini) hitting OpenAI directly, not OpenRouter, so M9 (judge on unconstrained route) does not apply here.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Abstract; Introduction bullet 1 (p.1); §8 Discussion "Model Type" table; Figure 2; Figure 8 "Distribution of number of steps"; Appendix F "Hacking breakdown" + F.3 (Figures 15-17)] Abstract/headline claim "DeepSeek R1 will often hack the benchmark by default"; Figure 2 "Baseline runs summary" DeepSeek R1 row (environment_failure 0.15, replace_stockfish 0.04, replace_board 0.08, n=52); Figure 8 step-count histogram; Appendix F.3 Figures 15-17; §8 Discussion table classifying DeepSeek R1 as a hacking "1st gen Reasoning Model"
    → mechanism: M3 probabilistic routing + M1 unpinned quantization + M11 silent backend mixing, compounded by M4 no provenance logging. deepseek/deepseek-r1 is invoked via OpenRouter in player.py's Provider class with zero provider pinning anywhere in the repo (no `provider` dict / extra_body). The authors' own Appendix F states: "The DeepSeek agents have run for fewer steps because the inference API was unstable and we had trouble recording long runs... this underestimates DeepSeek's hacking success because it has fewer steps to work with," and explicitly flags DeepSeek R1's breakdown as "not directly comparable" to o1-preview's. Figure 8 shows R1's session-length distribution concentrated near 0 steps versus o1-preview's cluster at 15-30 — the classic signature of an unpinned multi-provider route silently bouncing between backends of inconsistent uptime/latency/quantization rather than a property of the model itself. This also invalidates the apples-to-apples model comparison (M8) the abstract's claim rests on, since o1-preview/o3/Claude were served through single, consistent, proprietary APIs while R1 was not.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Introduction (p.1, 3rd bullet); §4.5 "Other language models"; Figure 2 "Baseline runs summary"; Figure 18 "Unexpected behavior heatmap for all models" (Appendix G)] Introduction bullet: "o1-mini, o3-mini, and QwQ 32B Preview can't use the game environment effectively"; §4.5: "QwQ 32B Preview... wasn't able to follow the agent instructions properly"; Figure 2 / Figure 18 QwQ 32B Preview environment_failure = 1.00 (baseline, n=49) and 0.98 (spooky, n=48), with 0.00 on every other category
    → mechanism: M1 unpinned quantization + M2 silent param dropping + M3 probabilistic routing. qwen/qwq-32b-preview is a bare, undated open-weight slug in player.py's OPENROUTER_MODELS set with no `provider` dict anywhere in the repo, so each call could land on any OpenRouter backend at any quantization (down to int4), and any backend could silently strip/alter the harness's default temperature=0.5 or mishandle its XML-tag-structured prompting. A near-total (100%/98%) environment_failure classification is exactly the failure signature a degraded/quantized or param-stripped backend would produce (malformed <ACTION>/<PLAN> tags the BeautifulSoup parser can't find). Unlike DeepSeek R1, the paper does not discuss API instability for QwQ and offers no per-call provenance to rule this out, so this is a plausible but author-unconfirmed confound rather than a demonstrated one — the 32B model's genuine incapability is also a real possibility.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: Pin OpenRouter routing for the DeepSeek/QwQ/Gemini rollouts (extra_body={"provider": {"order": [...], "allow_fallbacks": False, "quantizations": [...], "data_collection": "deny"}}), persist the served-provider/generation-id per call into the run's state dumps (not just an overwritten /tmp file), and disclose this as a Limitation alongside the existing judge-reliability caveat — since DeepSeek R1's "hacks by default" result is compared directly against pinned, direct-API proprietary models (o3, o1-preview) in the paper's abstract and headline discussion table.
Venue: arXiv (ICML-format writeup); widely covered by Time/Fortune/Business Insider and heavily cited in AI-control/scheming discourse
Provider A/B rerun assessment: VIABLE (one-line edit); amplification 7/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding, long-context retrieval, multi-turn compounding). Pin: Provider.__init__ (player.py:59-84) creates `self.client = OpenAI(base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1", api_key=os.environ["OPENROUTER_API_KEY"])` for any model in `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (player.py:33-38, includes "deepseek/deepseek-chat" and "deepseek/deepseek-r1" verbatim). All requests funnel thr... Model(s): deepseek/deepseek-r1, deepseek/deepseek-chat.

Title: diffing-toolkit (science-of-finetuning / Neel Nanda, Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, Stewy Slocum — MATS 7)
Summary: A general-purpose "model diffing" mech-interp toolkit (activation-difference lens, patchscope, logit-diff mining, steering) used to produce the Alignment Forum post's finding that narrow finetuning leaves clearly readable traces; it runs a local white-box pipeline on open-weight finetuned models (Qwen/Llama/Gemma/DeepSeek) and layers an LLM-based "interpretability agent" plus several LLM-judge graders on top to interpret/score the pipeline's outputs.
Importance/Impact: Co-authored by Neel Nanda (Google DeepMind interpretability team lead, one of the most prominent and widely-cited mech-interp researchers/educators in the field) along with Julian Minder, Clément Dumas, and Stewy Slocum (MATS 7). Repo has 78 GitHub stars and is an actively developed general-purpose 'model diffing' research toolkit (activation-difference lens, patch-scope grading, LLM-judge graders) used to produce the post's headline result about narrow finetuning leaving activation-level traces.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is the sole backend for (1) the interpretability agent (AgentLLM, src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py) that autonomously investigates and hypothesizes about model diffs, and (2) four LLM-judge grader classes (HypothesisGrader, CoherenceGrader, TokenRelevanceGrader, PatchScopeGrader) that score the agent's hypotheses, steered-generation coherence, token relevance, and patch-scope scale selection — all feeding numbers reported in the post. The models being diffed themselves (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, DeepSeek finetunes) are loaded locally via HuggingFace/nnsight/vLLM and never touch OpenRouter.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo uses OpenRouter exclusively to call proprietary, single-vendor-served frontier models (OpenAI GPT-5 family, Google Gemini 2.5 family, Anthropic Claude Haiku 4.5) as the agent/judge backend, so M1 (quantization), M3 (probabilistic provider load-balancing), and M8 (cross-provider confound) do not meaningfully apply — these vendors' own models are not multi-provider-quantized on OpenRouter the way open-weight models are, and the repo never routes an open-weight model through OpenRouter (those are loaded locally via HF/nnsight). No response_format/JSON-schema constrained output is used anywhere (graders parse free text like "SCORE: <n>" / "COHERENT"), so M9 doesn't apply either. However, three real, verifiable gaps remain and all sit on a critical route (the grader/agent outputs feed the paper's reported coherence percentages, hypothesis-quality scores, token-relevance scores, and patch-scope scale selections): (1) M5 — `data_collection` is never set anywhere in the codebase, so it defaults to OpenRouter's "allow," meaning prompts containing the model-diffing artifacts under study (patchscope decodings, steered generations, activation-difference summaries) may be retained/used for training by whichever provider serves the call. (2) M6 — every grader/agent model is a bare, undated slug (`openai/gpt-5`, `openai/gpt-5-mini`, `openai/gpt-5-nano`, `google/gemini-2.5-pro`, etc.); if OpenAI/Google/Anthropic update the model behind these aliases (plausible for actively-iterated frontier models), a rerun would silently score with a different judge, threatening exact reproduction of the reported grading numbers. (3) M4 — no code anywhere stores which provider actually served a call, a completion/generation id, or any other provenance; combined with M6 this means drift in grader behavior over time would be undetectable and undiagnosable after the fact. A weaker, lower-confidence note: AgentLLM always sends `temperature=0.7` (and CoherenceGrader occasionally `temperature=0`) with no `require_parameters:true`, so if OpenRouter ever silently ignores temperature for a given proprietary route the agent's exploration diversity could change unnoticed (M2) — plausible but not confirmed, and low-impact since OpenAI's own chat-completions API has long supported temperature. None of this threatens the paper's primary, white-box evidence (activation-difference lens / logit-lens / patch-scope analysis on locally-loaded open-weight models), which is the strongest support for the headline claim; the OpenRouter-based agent/judge layer is a secondary interpretive/scoring layer, and the authors do build in some redundancy (a multi-judge "grader_model_id" robustness sweep across OpenAI/Google/Anthropic judges analyzed via a hierarchical Bayesian model, plus `num_repeat` resampling) that would tend to surface — though not fully diagnose — gross judge instability.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The post's central claim — that the mech-interp agent (ADL, using an OpenRouter-routed openai/gpt-5 hypothesis-generator + openai/gpt-5-mini judge) dramatically outperforms a blackbox baseline (e.g. 97%/60% score≥2/≥4 at 5 interactions vs 12%/6% for the 50-interaction blackbox baseline) — was produced by unpinned OpenRouter model aliases with no response-side provenance logging, across a pipeline whose configs were actively edited over a multi-month window, so a silent vendor-side snapshot change between the ADL and baseline runs cannot be ruled out or even detected after the fact.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[AF post 'Headline Results' section (Agent Performance Comparison); generated by narrow_ft_experiments/plotting/plot_agent_performance.py (variants agent_mi0/agent_mi5/baseline_mi0/mi5/mi50) from data graded via configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml] Agent Performance Comparison (headline): ADL agent, 5 interactions: 97% reached score >=2, 60% reached >=4; ADL agent, 0 interactions: 94% >=2, 39% >=4; Blackbox baseline, 50 interactions: 12% >=2, 6% >=4 (1-5 hypothesis-grading rubric)
    → mechanism: M6 (version drift) + M4 (no provenance logging). configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml pins agent.llm.model_id to the unversioned OpenRouter alias 'openai/gpt-5' (a commented-out alternative 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4' shows the field was manually swapped at times) and grader.model_id to 'openai/gpt-5-mini' -- neither is a dated/pinned snapshot. Git history on that file shows active edits from 2025-11-04 through 2026-02-05 (interaction budgets changed, baselines toggled on/off, grader runs added), so the ADL and blackbox-baseline conditions being compared were very plausibly produced in separate sessions spread over months. Neither src/diffing/utils/agents/llm.py (AgentLLM.chat) nor src/diffing/utils/graders/grader.py (Grader._call_with_retry) records the actual served model id / provider metadata returned by OpenRouter (e.g. completion.model, system_fingerprint) -- only response text and token usage are kept. If OpenAI silently rolled the default 'gpt-5'/'gpt-5-mini' snapshot between when the ADL condition and the blackbox-baseline condition were run, the method-vs-baseline gap would be confounded with a model-version difference, and this is now unauditable.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[AF post 'Mitigation Through Training Data' section] Data-mixing mitigation ablation: mixing pretraining samples 1:1 with finetuning data drops the agent's hypothesis-grading score to 1 (near-failure) versus 3 on unmixed data
    → mechanism: M6 + M4, same as above: this ablation runs through the identical agent (openai/gpt-5) + HypothesisGrader (openai/gpt-5-mini) OpenRouter pipeline defined in configs/diffing/evaluation.yaml, and inherits the same unpinned-alias / no-provenance-logging exposure. Given the pipeline's grading rubrics and evaluation config were edited repeatedly across the Nov 2025-Feb 2026 window (per commit history), the mixed and unmixed conditions were likely run in different sessions, so a mid-collection snapshot change on either 'gpt-5' or 'gpt-5-mini' could shift the 1-vs-3 gap without being detectable from stored logs.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[AF post 'Headline Findings' -- Patchscope Token Relevance claim] Patchscope Token Relevance: 'the maximum fraction of relevant tokens in top-20 Patchscope outputs [is] significantly higher than the baselines' across all organism (finetune) types
    → mechanism: M6 + M4: TokenRelevanceGrader is configured in configs/diffing/method/activation_difference_lens.yaml with grader.model_id: openai/gpt-5-mini, base_url https://openrouter.ai/api/v1 -- again an unpinned alias, and the same Grader base class (src/diffing/utils/graders/grader.py) discards any served-model/provider metadata from the OpenRouter response. This claim aggregates across many organism conditions (see the ~80 configs/organism/*.yaml files) almost certainly graded at different times over the multi-month development window, so any silent 'gpt-5-mini' snapshot change would unevenly affect the per-organism relevance scores being compared.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[narrow_ft_experiments/hibayes/agent_grades/grader_agreement.py, producing tab:grader_agreement (LaTeX table exported to grader_agreement.tex) -- a judge-reliability/robustness check likely underlying reported inter-grader agreement] Grader cross-model agreement / reliability statistics (Pearson correlation per grader pair and Krippendorff's ordinal alpha) computed between GPT-5-mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Claude Haiku 4.5 as judges on the same hypothesis samples
    → mechanism: M6 + M4: all three grader backends (openai/gpt-5-mini, google/gemini-2.5-flash, anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5) are called through the identical unpinned-alias, provenance-blind OpenRouter path in src/diffing/utils/graders/grader.py. This analysis exists specifically to establish that the judge is reliable/model-agnostic, but if any of the three vendors silently updated their default snapshot mid-collection, the reported correlation/alpha would reflect a partly-nonstationary rater rather than three fixed raters -- undermining the very robustness claim the statistic is meant to support, in an unknown direction.
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
One-line fix: Set `provider: {data_collection: "deny", require_parameters: true}` in every OpenRouter config and log the response's served-provider / generation id alongside each agent and grader call (e.g. persist `completion.id` and call `GET /api/v1/generation?id=...`), and pin/record the exact dated model snapshot actually used for the agent and each grader.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 7/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding, LLM-as-judge score drift). Blocker: The entire headline experiment (paper 'Narrow Finetuning Leaves Clearly Readable Traces', arXiv:2510.13900) requires local GPUs to host paired base+finetuned model organisms (Gemma3-1B, Qwen3-1.7B/32B, Llama-3.2-1B(-Instruct), Gemma2-9B-it, Qwen2.5-7B/VL-3B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Title: lighteval — Swiss-Legal / LEXam LLM-as-judge tasks (JudgeLM litellm backend)
Summary: lighteval is Hugging Face's official eval library (2479 stars, powers the Open LLM Leaderboard). Its `swiss_legal` task module — shipped for the ICLR 2026 LEXam benchmark and companion Swiss-legal tasks (SLDS summarization, SLT translation) — scores model outputs with an LLM-as-judge, and several of the default/sweep judge-model configs are routed through OpenRouter with zero routing safeguards.
Importance/Impact: lighteval has 2479 GitHub stars and is Hugging Face's official evaluation library, used to produce numbers for the Open LLM Leaderboard and many papers. The LEXam benchmark (integrated directly into lighteval per the HF blog 'The state-of-the-art in open-source AI for Swiss legal tasks') was accepted at ICLR 2026 and ships its paper-grounded LLM-as-judge metric inside lighteval.
What they use OpenRouter for: LLM-as-judge backend for two of the three Swiss-legal task families: (1) SLDS (Swiss Landmark Decision Summarization) — the *default* judge is `openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat` (open-weight DeepSeek-V3), used unconditionally by `_get_metrics()` for every SLDS subtask; (2) SLT (Swiss Legal Translation) — the `JUDGE_MODELS` sweep routes o1, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-4o, and llama-3.3-70b-instruct all through `openrouter/...` slugs, and `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` (metrics.py:980) makes this sweep the default metric set for the task. In both cases the judge score IS the reported corpus-level metric (`corpus_level_fn=statistics.mean`), not an intermediate/exploratory signal. By contrast, the paper's flagship LEXam open-question judge (`get_lexam_oq_judge()`, main.py:421) defaults to `openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20` with no `openrouter/` prefix — litellm routes that directly to OpenAI, not through OpenRouter — so the headline LEXam OQ/MCQ numbers are likely unaffected; the exposure is in the companion SLDS/SLT benchmarks living in the same module.
Mistakes (null if safe): The judge-call path shared by all Swiss-legal metrics is `JudgeLM.__call_litellm` (src/lighteval/metrics/utils/llm_as_judge.py:305-371). Its kwargs dict is `{"model", "messages", "n": 1, "caching": True, "response_format": self.response_format}` plus `max_tokens` — no `provider` key at all, so every "openrouter/"-prefixed model gets OpenRouter's default behavior: no `quantizations` pin (M1), no `order`/`only`/`sort` so requests load-balance across providers (M3), no `data_collection: "deny"` so prompts (Swiss court judgments/legal exam text) default to "allow" at providers that train on data (M5), and the function returns only `response.choices[0].message.content` (line 348/352) — the served provider / generation id is never captured or stored anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via `grep -rIn` for extra_body/require_parameters/quantizations/data_collection/generation_id across src/: zero hits) (M4). Concretely: `get_swiss_landmark_decision_summarization_judge(..., model_name: str = "openrouter/deepseek/deepseek-chat", ...)` (metrics.py:795) is the *unconditional default* judge for every SLDS headnote-quality score (wired in via `main.py:320-322`, no override) — DeepSeek-V3 behind a bare, undated alias (`deepseek-chat`, M6) served by whichever of DeepSeek's many OpenRouter providers/quantizations wins that request, with the SLDS score being the exact number reported for that model/language. Separately, `JUDGE_MODELS` (metrics.py:928-932) maps `"o1"→"openrouter/openai/o1"`, `"gpt-4o-mini"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18"`, `"gpt-4o"→"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"`, `"llama-3-3-70b"→"openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct"`, and with `metrics_to_evaluate = ["judge"]` hardcoded (metrics.py:980), all of these feed `JUDGE_METRICS` and become the default SLT-judge metric sweep — comparing a proprietary trio against an unpinned open-weight 70B judge with identical (absent) safeguards on both sides (M8: any "which judge best matches humans" conclusion drawn from this sweep is confounded by DeepSeek/Llama quantization variance that GPT-4o/o1 don't share). Note also `init_llm_judge_metric` (metrics.py:1054-1058) builds `"slt_judge_gpt_4o"` from `judge_model_name="openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` (direct OpenAI, no "openrouter/" prefix) while `JUDGE_MODELS["gpt-4o"]` builds the *same* checkpoint through `"openrouter/openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20"` — the identical model string is called via two different backends in the same file (M11 silent backend mixing); the inline comment at metrics.py:929 ("Updated to match OpenRouter availability; previously: openai/o1-2024-12-17") confirms this migration happened without adding any pinning/provenance. Finally, neither the repo's README/docs nor the paper abstract (checked via WebFetch on arxiv.org/abs/2505.12864) mention OpenRouter, provider routing, or judge-output reproducibility at all (M10) — the only two files in the repo mentioning "openrouter" are the ones cited above, and both are purely functional (routing the call / estimating context length via OpenRouter's endpoints API), never provenance-related. Threatened results: the SLDS headnote-quality scores and the SLT judge-agreement/quality comparisons across judge models — both of which are the literal `corpus_level_fn` numbers lighteval reports for these Swiss-legal tasks. The LEXam paper's own headline open-question judge score is not implicated by this specific defect since its default (`openai/gpt-4o-2024-11-20`, no "openrouter/" prefix) resolves to direct OpenAI, not OpenRouter.

Title: safety-tooling (safety-research org shared alignment-research inference library)
Summary: A shared, general-purpose async LLM inference wrapper (`InferenceAPI`) maintained by the safety-research/MATS/Anthropic-Fellows ecosystem and depended on by dozens of downstream alignment papers/repos to run model generations, evals, and data generation. It routes calls to many backends (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Together, HuggingFace, vLLM, DeepSeek-direct, and OpenRouter) behind one uniform `InferenceAPI.__call__` interface.
Importance/Impact: 132 stars, but importance is structural rather than star-count: this is the shared, general-purpose LLM inference library maintained by the `safety-research` org (the Anthropic Fellows Program / MATS alignment-research ecosystem) and reused as a dependency across dozens of downstream alignment/control repos in that org (persona_vectors, false-facts, science-synth-facts, believe-it-or-not, introspection-mechanisms, SHADE-Arena's sibling projects, etc.), making it high-leverage infra for a large fraction of independent/MATS-affiliated safety research output.
What they use OpenRouter for: Provides `OpenRouterChatModel` (safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py), auto-selected by `InferenceAPI.model_id_to_class()` (safetytooling/apis/inference/api.py:312-313) whenever `model_id` is in the hardcoded `OPENROUTER_MODELS` set or starts with `"openrouter/"`, or via `force_provider="openrouter"`. `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (openrouter.py:15-30) is mostly open-weight models: `meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b(:free)`, `deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free`, `deepseek/deepseek-r1-zero:free`, `nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-{70b,405b}`, `mistralai/mistral-*`, plus some proprietary-via-router entries (`x-ai/grok-4`, `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001`). README.md also documents a second path: calling OpenRouter through the plain `OpenAIChatModel` via `openai_base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` + `force_provider="openai"` (README.md:150-161) — a different code path than the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class for the same underlying endpoint. Output (`LLMResponse.completion`, logprobs, etc.) is consumed by any downstream repo built on this infra for rollouts/evals/data generation, i.e. it is the generic model-calling substrate for a large share of independent alignment research.
Mistakes (null if safe): This is shared infra, so the corrupting mistakes threaten every downstream repo's numbers that route open-weight models (Llama-3.1-405b, DeepSeek-v3/r1, Mistral-large, Hermes-3) through the default `OpenRouterChatModel`/`force_provider="openrouter"` path. M1 (no `quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase): any eval/rollout/data-generation run against these open-weight models can silently be served at a different quantization each run, threatening reproducibility of any benchmark or generated dataset built on it. M2 (no `require_parameters`): sampling params like `temperature`, `logprobs`, `seed`, and arbitrary kwargs (LLMParams allows `extra="allow"`, e.g. `response_format`) are passed straight through with no guarantee the serving provider honors them — `test_openrouter_logprobs` even relies on `logprobs`+`temperature=0.0` being honored, unchecked. M3 (no `order`/`only`/`sort`): every call is subject to OpenRouter's default load-balanced routing, so repeated calls to the same "model_id" for a comparison or ablation study can hit different providers run-to-run. M4 (no provenance logging): `LLMResponse` has no field for which provider/generation actually served a call, so a downstream paper cannot audit after the fact which backend produced any given cached completion — this alone makes any claim about reproducibility of OpenRouter-derived results unverifiable. M5 (`data_collection` never set, defaults to "allow"): alignment-research prompts (which can include sensitive red-teaming/deception content) sent through this shared path may be used for provider training by default. M8 (cross-model comparison confound): this infra exists specifically to let downstream papers compare many models under one interface (that is its purpose across the alignment ecosystem); since none of the OpenRouter-routed open-weight models are pinned, any downstream cross-model comparison inherits unpinned provider/quantization variance as a confound. M10 (no reporting): README/CLAUDE.md/REPO_GUIDE.md never mention OpenRouter's provider-variance/quantization risk, so downstream authors adopting this infra are not warned to add safeguards themselves. M11 (silent backend mixing): the README's own documented alternative (OpenAIChatModel pointed at OpenRouter's base_url) and the dedicated OpenRouterChatModel class are two different code paths that can both serve the same "model" family via OpenRouter, with different kwargs-handling and error-parsing behavior, inviting inconsistent treatment of the same nominal model across a project.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: safety-tooling's default `OpenRouterChatModel` route — auto-selected via `model_id_to_class()` for the ~13 open-weight model IDs hardcoded in `OPENROUTER_MODELS` (e.g. `meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b`, `meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct`, `mistralai/mistral-large-2411`, `deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free`, `nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b`) — has no quantization/provider pinning, no provenance logging (`cost=0` is hardcoded, no served-provider field is ever captured), and no filtering of pass-through sampling kwargs anywhere in the ~400-line `openrouter.py`, so every downstream eval/rollout/judge run that calls `InferenceAPI` with one of these model IDs silently inherits non-reproducible, unaudited backend routing.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py L17-33 (OPENROUTER_MODELS set); safetytooling/apis/inference/api.py model_id_to_class(), "elif model_id in OPENROUTER_MODELS or model_id.startswith('openrouter/'): return self._openrouter" branch] The hardcoded OPENROUTER_MODELS set — meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b, meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b:free, meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, mistralai/mistral-large-2411, mistralai/mistral-small-24b-instruct-2501, mistral/ministral-8b, deepseek/deepseek-v3-base:free, deepseek/deepseek-r1-zero:free, nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-70b, nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b — is the library's silent default route to OpenRouter for any downstream repo's model_id argument.
    → mechanism: M1 (no quantizations filter anywhere in the codebase) + M3 (probabilistic routing). Confirmed by reading the full 399-line openrouter.py: the only kwargs manipulation is converting logprobs->top_logprobs; kwargs are otherwise passed unmodified to aclient.chat.completions.create(**kwargs) with no default provider/quantization constraint. Any run against these open-weight IDs can be served at a different quantization (down to int4) or by a different upstream inference engine on every call, non-reproducibly.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py L25-32 (comment above the 6 model IDs in OPENROUTER_MODELS)] Six model IDs (meta-llama/llama-3.1-8b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct, meta-llama/llama-3.1-405b-instruct, nousresearch/hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b, mistralai/mistral-small-24b-instruct-2501) are labeled in a source comment "# DeepInfra models accessible through OpenRouter", implying the developers believed these were pinned to a single known upstream provider — but no provider.order/allow_fallbacks parameter is ever set to enforce that.
    → mechanism: M1 + M11 (silent backend mixing). The comment documents an intent (route to DeepInfra) that the code never enforces, so OpenRouter's default load balancer can route these IDs to any provider currently serving them (Novita, Together, Fireworks, etc.), each potentially at different quantization/precision, invisibly under the same model_id string across runs of the same downstream experiment.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py, LLMResponse(...) constructions inside __call__ (~L340-390); no `provider` field is populated anywhere in the file] LLMResponse objects returned by OpenRouterChatModel.__call__ hardcode cost=0 and never capture which upstream provider or quantized variant actually served the request, for both the n>1 and n=1 response-construction branches.
    → mechanism: M4 (no provenance logging). Even the fact that a distortion occurred (quantization change, provider switch) is unrecoverable after the fact for any downstream repo, because the wrapper discards the routing metadata OpenRouter would otherwise expose. This forecloses retroactive auditing of every OpenRouter-routed result the library has ever produced.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[tests/test_other_apis.py L74-98 (test_openrouter_call, force_provider="openrouter"); REPO_GUIDE.md L293/L314 (force_provider documentation)] force_provider="openrouter" — used at the primary call site tests/test_other_apis.py L82-98 (test_openrouter_call, the exact call site cited in this task) and available to every downstream caller — only selects the OpenRouterChatModel *class*; it is not an OpenRouter provider/quantization pin, despite REPO_GUIDE.md L314 describing force_provider generically as a way to "force use of a specific provider."
    → mechanism: M3 + M10. A downstream researcher who copies this pattern to "pin reproducibility" is not pinning anything upstream of OpenRouter's own load balancer — the naming makes it easy to mistake provider-class selection (safety-tooling picks OpenRouter's API) for provider/quantization selection (which specific inference host serves the weights), so the mistake goes unreported.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[safetytooling/apis/inference/openrouter.py __call__ (no response_format-specific code path exists in the file)] response_format / structured-output kwargs are documented generically as pass-through ("response_format (for OpenAI)", REPO_GUIDE.md L316) but OpenRouterChatModel has no special handling, validation, or fallback check for structured-output support when a call is routed to one of the OPENROUTER_MODELS open-weight IDs.
    → mechanism: M9 (judge on unconstrained route). If any downstream repo uses one of these OpenRouter-routed model IDs as an LLM judge with a structured-output/JSON-schema constraint, and OpenRouter's router sends that particular call to a provider that ignores response_format, the constraint can be silently dropped server-side with nothing in safety-tooling to detect it.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: In `OpenRouterChatModel.__call__` (openrouter.py), default every request to `extra_body={"provider": {"quantizations": ["fp8","fp16","bf16","fp32"], "require_parameters": True, "data_collection": "deny", "sort": "exacto"}}` (overridable per-call), and add a `provider`/`generation_id` field to `LLMResponse` populated from the raw `response_data` so every cached completion records exactly which backend served it.
Venue: Multiple (shared infra: Anthropic Fellows Program / MATS alignment-research ecosystem)
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 1/10 (no strong amplification mechanism). Blocker: No headline experiment exists in this repo. README.md, REPO_GUIDE.md (auto-generated repo tour), and CLAUDE.md all describe safety-tooling uniformly as a shared LLM-API *library* (unified InferenceAPI wrapper over OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini/Together/OpenRouter/etc, caching, batch API, finetuning helpers) — there is no paper, benchmark, eval ...

Title: Evaluating LLMs for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations — experiment code (openai/hallucinations-paper-experiments)
Summary: Official OpenAI repo backing the Nature 2026 paper (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang) showing accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination; a Jupyter notebook queries 4 frontier LLMs on the full SimpleQA test set under varying abstention penalties and grades every response with GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge, all via OpenRouter.
Importance/Impact: Official OpenAI-authored repo (org: openai) for a paper published in Nature (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang, 2026), 'Evaluating large language models for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations' — a flagship OpenAI research result on why accuracy-only grading incentivizes hallucination. High-authority source (OpenAI research team) and high-visibility venue.
What they use OpenRouter for: Both legs of the pipeline go through OpenRouter's /chat/completions endpoint via lm.py's LM.generate(): (1) generation — querying google/gemini-3-pro-preview, openai/gpt-5, x-ai/grok-4, anthropic/claude-opus-4.5 on all 4,326 SimpleQA questions x multiple abstention rewards x baseline/consistency-mitigation conditions x 2 seeds each; (2) grading — every answer is graded CORRECT/INCORRECT/NOT_ATTEMPTED by openai/gpt-4.1 using the SimpleQA grader template (free-text A/B/C output, retried until valid). Both outputs feed directly into the paper's reported hallucination/abstention/accuracy figures (figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf, figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf, figures/full_table.tex).
Mistakes (null if safe): All 4 subject models plus the GPT-4.1 grader are proprietary, effectively lab-single-served on OpenRouter, so the highest-severity multi-provider risks (M1 quantization, M3 probabilistic routing, M8 cross-model comparison confound) are essentially N/A — no `provider` dict is set anywhere (grepped whole repo, zero hits), but that mostly doesn't matter for these models. Two real issues remain that DO threaten reported numbers: (1) M4 — lm.py never records which provider served a call or the OpenRouter generation id; `output_mode="full"` (the only mode that would surface OpenRouter's `provider` field) is defined in lm.py:248-249 but never invoked anywhere in experiment.ipynb (grepped: only "with_usage" and default "response" modes are used), so even the modest residual risk for Claude (which OpenRouter can route via Anthropic-direct, Bedrock, or Vertex) is unverifiable after the fact. (2) M6 — a genuine, verified discrepancy: README.md:31 states the GPT condition queried was `openai/gpt-5.2`, but lm.py:42-45 defines the "gpt-5" key as bare `openai/gpt-5` (no date/version suffix), and this is exactly what the notebook actually calls (experiment.ipynb cell 2: `"gpt": LM("gpt-5")`) — confirmed by the checked-in execution trace in EXAMPLES.md:442 (`choice: 1 gpt-5`, not gpt-5.2). This means the repo's stated "reproduce the paper's experiments" claim is not accurate for the GPT condition as committed: a re-run today queries whatever `openai/gpt-5` currently bare-resolves to, which is not verifiably the same checkpoint that produced the published GPT curve in figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf and figures/full_table.tex, and could drift further if OpenAI repoints the alias. M2 (param dropping) and M9 (judge on unconstrained route) were considered but don't really apply: the grader uses free-text A/B/C output with client-side validation/retry (no response_format/schema requested at all), and the few sampling params passed (temperature=0, max_tokens=16, seed) are basic, universally-supported OpenAI params on an OpenAI-only route. M5 (data_collection left "allow") is technically true (never set) but low real-world impact since SimpleQA questions are public benchmark data, not sensitive.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The paper's headline qualitative claim (closed/accuracy-only grading drives higher hallucination rates than open-rubric grading, and consistency-mitigation reduces it) is unlikely to reverse, since it comes from within-model contrasts, not cross-provider comparisons; but the exact reported percentages in the main results table and figures cannot be verified as reproducible, because lm.py logs no provider/model-snapshot provenance for any OpenRouter call, and the repo's own README already shows a live version-label mismatch (cost table says "GPT-5.2" was queried; the code registry actually requests OpenRouter id "openai/gpt-5" for that same run).
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[figures/full_table.tex (headline results table)] Main results table (figures/full_table.tex): per-model accuracy/hallucination-rate matrix across 4 penalty thresholds (L=0,1,3,9) x {Closed, Open} rubric x {baseline, mitigated}, e.g. LLM2 (GPT) closed-rubric baseline at threshold 0% = 0.49, LLM4 (Opus) closed-rubric baseline at threshold 0% = 0.37, LLM1 (Gemini) open-rubric mitigated at threshold 90% = 0.88
    → mechanism: M4 (no provenance logging) + M6 (version drift): lm.py's _generate() posts bare, unpinned model aliases ('google/gemini-3-pro-preview', 'openai/gpt-5', 'x-ai/grok-4', 'anthropic/claude-opus-4.5') with no `provider` dict anywhere in the repo (verified: zero hits for a routing/provider field in lm.py, the notebook, README, or EXAMPLES.md). The code path used throughout (output_mode='with_usage') actively discards the returned completion's `is_byok` and `cost_details` fields before storage, so even the metadata OpenRouter does return is thrown away -- there is no way after the fact to confirm which exact checkpoint/provider served any of the ~138k+ calls behind this table. Concretely: the README's own cost table names 'GPT-5.2' as the model queried, while lm.py's OPENROUTER_MODELS registry maps the 'gpt-5' key used by the notebook to the OpenRouter id 'openai/gpt-5' (not gpt-5.2) -- a documented code/doc mismatch that is itself an instance of M6.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf (notebook cell 19, plot_paired_halluc_rates)] Figure figures/simpleqa_halluc_rates.pdf: paired baseline-vs-consistency-mitigated hallucination-rate plot across all 4 models (LLM1-4)
    → mechanism: Same as the main table (M4 no provenance + M6 alias/version drift): this figure is generated directly from the same unpinned, unlogged OpenRouter generate()/grade_answer() calls, so any mid-collection snapshot change behind a 'preview'-tagged or bare alias (e.g. gemini-3-pro-preview, or the gpt-5/gpt-5.2 discrepancy) would be silently baked into these points with no audit trail.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf (notebook cell 22)] Figure figures/simpleqa_abstention.pdf: abstention-rate-vs-threshold curves with 95% bootstrap CIs, open-rubric (solid) vs closed-rubric baseline (dashed) for all 4 models
    → mechanism: Same mechanism as above (M4 + M6): curves are built from the identical unpinned/unlogged OpenRouter LM.generate() and grade_answer() (GPT-4.1 judge) calls, so provenance of exactly which snapshot/provider produced each point is unverifiable post hoc.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium

[notebook cells 33-34 (MINIS section)] GPT-5-mini vs o4-mini secondary check: closed/open-rubric (CR@0/OR@0) accuracy printed as 'redoing what is in the system cards to confirm', plus paired permutation-test p-values comparing the two models on OR@0 and CR@0
    → mechanism: M4 (no provenance) + M6 (version drift), specifically acute for o4-mini: it is an older OpenAI model whose default OpenRouter alias route/backend could have shifted by the time this run executed, and -- as with the gpt-5/gpt-5.2 case above -- there is no logged snapshot id to confirm the OpenRouter-served checkpoint matches what OpenAI's own system card team benchmarked. Both models are OpenAI-proprietary so cross-provider quantization (M1) risk is low; the exposure is specifically an unverifiable model-identity match for this named 'confirm the system card' claim and the head-to-head p-value.

Title: Inspect AI (UK AI Safety Institute)
Summary: Inspect AI is UKAISI's open-source LLM evaluation harness (the substrate METR, Apollo, inspect_evals, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers run rollouts and scoring through). It ships a first-class `OpenRouterAPI(OpenAICompatibleAPI)` provider alongside ~15 direct providers.
Importance/Impact: The standard eval harness for the AI safety/control field: 2,369 GitHub stars, 609 forks, built and maintained by UK AISI. It underlies inspect_evals and is used directly or via wrappers by METR (hawk, inspect-agents, inspect-action), Apollo Research, and many published scheming/sandbagging/control papers as the execution substrate for model rollouts and scoring.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable model backends: `inspect eval task.py --model openrouter/org/model` routes chat-completion calls for eval/agent rollouts and (via user-configured judge models) scoring through OpenRouter's OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
Mistakes (null if safe): As shipped, the documented/idiomatic OpenRouter usage path has no default safeguards: (1) M1 no quantizations pin — open-weight model evals (a large fraction of what OpenRouter routes, e.g. Llama/DeepSeek/Mixtral variants used in capability/safety evals) can silently run at int4/int8 on one backend vs bf16 on another, confounding any capability or control-evaluation score computed via Inspect+OpenRouter run-to-run or across papers. (2) M2/M7 no require_parameters — sampling params (temperature, seed, etc.) can be silently dropped by whichever provider OpenRouter load-balances to; the library's own `seed` docstring confirms OpenRouter isn't in the list of providers where seed is honored. (3) M3 no order/only/sort — provider selection is probabilistic by default, so repeated eval runs (or the same eval across two papers using the same model string) may hit materially different backends/quantizations, threatening reproducibility of any capability/safety score. (4) M5 data_collection is never set/documented, so safety-sensitive eval transcripts (e.g. red-team prompts, scheming/sandbagging rollouts) default to whatever OpenRouter account-level data policy is in force, unflagged to the researcher. Because Inspect is described as the execution substrate for rollouts/scoring across a wide swath of the safety-eval ecosystem, any downstream paper that follows the basic README example without additionally learning and setting OpenRouter's `provider={...}` object inherits all four gaps. The one significant mitigant is that Inspect logs the full raw response (including OpenRouter's actual-provider and generation-id fields) into every `.eval` transcript by default, so drift is at least auditable after the fact even though it isn't prevented.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: In the UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals PersistBench reproduction (run entirely through Inspect AI's default, unpinned OpenRouterAPI path), the measured cross-domain-leakage failure_rate for `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` is 25.0, which falls OUTSIDE the PersistBench paper's reported 95% bootstrap CI of [12.0, 23.0] (arXiv:2602.01146) — a discrepancy plausibly explained by silent quantization/provider variance on the unpinned route.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md, 'Evaluation Report' table, row 1 (also 2026-05-22 archived .eval JSON results/persistbench_beneficial_memory/2026-05-22_openrouter_meta-llama_llama-3.3-70b-instruct.json for the sibling beneficial_memory run)] PersistBench `persistbench_cross_domain` failure_rate for `openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` = 25.0, outside the paper's 95% CI [12.0, 23.0]
    → mechanism: M1 (no quantization pin) + M3 (probabilistic routing). Verified directly in the raw .eval log: eval.model='openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct', eval.model_args={}, plan.config={} — i.e. zero provider/quantization constraints were sent. This traces to inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py: `self.provider = collect_model_arg('provider')` (L108-112) defaults to None and is only injected into `extra_body['provider']` inside `completion_params()` (L372-383) when the caller explicitly passes `-M provider={...}`. The reproduction command in the README (`inspect eval-set ... --model openrouter/meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct`) never does this, so OpenRouter was free to route the 70B open-weight model to any registered inference provider at any precision (int4/int8/fp8/bf16) it had capacity for, and to a different provider on a different run — a plausible source of the out-of-CI drift vs. the paper's presumably fixed inference stack.

[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/README.md 'Evaluation Report' table (all 6 rows) and src/inspect_evals/persistbench/report.md] Remaining 5 persistbench failure_rate figures for the same two open-weight subject models across the three tasks (e.g. sycophancy 85.0/99.0, beneficial_memory 54.0/22.0, cross_domain 53.0 for gpt-oss-120b)
    → mechanism: M1 + M3, same as above — every subject-model row uses `openrouter/<org>/<open-weight-model>` with no `-M provider=...` model arg, so all six numbers were produced on the same unpinned default path. Several land inside the paper's CI on this particular run, but the underlying quantization/provider is not pinned or logged, so run-to-run reproducibility of any of these six numbers is not guaranteed (a re-run could silently land on a different backend/precision).

[inspect_evals repo: src/inspect_evals/persistbench/scorers.py (DEFAULT_JUDGE_MODEL, DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER) and README.md 'Scoring' section] Default PersistBench judge `openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2-thinking`, described as chosen because 'the paper's judge calibration was performed against Vertex AI-hosted Kimi K2 Thinking, ensuring score consistency with published results' — used to score all persistbench_* results
    → mechanism: Partial mitigation, residual M2 risk. The provider *routing* is correctly pinned (`DEFAULT_JUDGE_PROVIDER = {'order': ['google-vertex'], 'allow_fallbacks': False}`), which avoids M3/M8 cross-backend drift for the judge itself. But `require_parameters` is not set and no `quantizations` constraint is given, so `GenerateConfig(temperature=0, reasoning_effort='high')` + `reasoning_enabled=True` sent to the judge could be silently dropped/altered by OpenRouter/Vertex rather than erroring if any combination is unsupported, undermining the stated 'score consistency with published results' guarantee for every judged score in this benchmark.

[Inspect AI docs: providers.html 'OpenRouter' section (inspect.aisi.org.uk/providers.html#openrouter); code: src/inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py L95-135 (provider/models/transforms args default to None via collect_model_arg) and L334-385 (completion_params only adds extra_body['provider'] when self.provider is truthy)] The documented/idiomatic Inspect AI OpenRouter invocation pattern itself, `inspect eval <task> --model openrouter/<org>/<model>` (exact form used in Inspect's own quickstart example `inspect eval arc.py --model openrouter/gryphe/mythomax-l2-13b`), ships with no default quantization pin or require_parameters

This is the general class of downstream results instruction #4 asks about: any Inspect-run capability/safety eval, agent rollout, or LLM-judge score (across METR, Apollo Research, inspect_evals broadly, and published scheming/sandbagging/control papers) that specifies an open-weight model via `--model openrouter/<org>/<model>` without appending `-M provider='{"quantizations": [...], "require_parameters": true}'` inherits this exact unpinned path. Safeguards present: provenance_logging (automatic, via full raw response capture), quantizations (opt-in via provider dict, documented), order/only/sort (opt-in via provider dict, mentioned once for caching not reproducibility), require_parameters and data_collection (mechanism works via free-form dict passthrough but is completely undocumented — zero hits anywhere in repo)
