Provider A/B rerun assessment: Not a rerun target — it is a framework, not an experiment — but it is the REFERENCE IMPLEMENTATION for this whole exercise and the reason several other rows are zero-config. Verified in upstream source: OpenRouterAPI.__init__ collects a `provider` dict model arg and completion_params() emits it as extra_body['provider'], so `-M provider='{"only": ["cerebras/fp16"], "allow_fallbacks": false}'` pins any inspect-based eval with zero code changes. Its own source also documents cross-provider behavioural divergence: a comment notes that AkashML and Parasail reject requests with more than one system message even though the OpenAI API is permissive — corroboration that 'same model, different provider' is not only a quantization story.

Title: MathArena (ETH Zurich SRI Lab)
Summary: MathArena is a near-live evaluation harness/leaderboard that tests frontier LLMs on newly-released, contamination-free competition math problems (AIME, HMMT, USAMO, Putnam, etc.), publishing a widely-cited public leaderboard.
Importance/Impact: 271 stars; run by ETH Zurich's SRI lab, well-known for security/verification-adjacent ML research; MathArena's near-live tracking of model performance on brand-new competition math problems (avoiding training-data contamination) is frequently cited in AI-capability discourse as a trustworthy math benchmark.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (api: openrouter) is one of ~10 API backends the unified APIClient supports; it is used to query a specific subset of models -- Llama-4-Maverick, the Kimi K2 family (k2, k2-thinking, k2.5, k2.6), Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free, the Qwen3/Qwen3.5/Qwen3-VL family, and Grok-4 -- whose transcripts/scores feed directly into the public leaderboard (13 of 140 model configs use api: openrouter). Judges/graders (main_judge, arxiv_judge, simple_judge, norm_judge) all use direct Google/other APIs, never OpenRouter.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: MathArena's leaderboard rows for Kimi K2, Llama-4-Maverick, Phi-4-reasoning-plus, and (the original) Qwen3-235B-A22B are queried via OpenRouter with no functioning provider pin — for Llama-4-Maverick the pin that IS present in the YAML config is a code-verified no-op (it crashes the OpenAI SDK call rather than reaching OpenRouter), meaning these open-weight rows are exposed to unpinned/quantized/rotating-provider routing while being ranked head-to-head against proprietary and properly-pinned rows on the same public leaderboard.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[configs/models/moonshot/k2.yaml (api: openrouter, no provider/extra_body pin) — MathArena leaderboard entry "Kimi K2"] Kimi K2 leaderboard row (human_readable_id "Kimi K2", model moonshotai/kimi-k2)
    → mechanism: M1 (unpinned quantization) + M3 (probabilistic routing): the config has no `extra_body.provider` field at all — unlike its own sibling configs k2-thinking.yaml, k25.yaml and k26.yaml, all added later, which pin `allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]`. Kimi K2 (1T-param / 32B-active MoE) is hosted by many third-party OpenRouter providers at varying quantization; with no pin, OpenRouter's default load-balancer can silently serve this specific row from any of them, and can switch providers between runs of the same eval.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[configs/models/llama4/maverick.yaml (api: openrouter; top-level `provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [Fireworks]}` key) — MathArena leaderboard entry "Llama-4-Maverick"] Llama-4-Maverick leaderboard row (human_readable_id "Llama-4-Maverick", model meta-llama/llama-4-maverick)
    → mechanism: M1 + M3, but with a code-verified twist: the maintainers clearly intended to pin this model to Fireworks (README even documents `provider` as a supported config key), but this config is the ONLY one of 8 pinned OpenRouter configs in the repo that puts `provider` at the YAML top level instead of nested under `extra_body:` (compare configs/models/moonshot/k2-thinking.yaml, k25.yaml, k26.yaml and the qwen3_*_2507/vl/30b configs, which all correctly nest it as `extra_body: {provider: {...}}`). Neither `_kwarg_remover` (api_client.py L223-241) nor `_clean_client_args` (math_agent.py L77-81, math_static_agent.py, selfcheck_agent.py) strip or relocate a bare `provider` key, so it survives into `self.kwargs` and is unpacked directly into `client.chat.completions.create(**payload)` (api_client.py ~L1745). I installed the repo's pinned minimum `openai>=1.102.0` and confirmed empirically that `Completions.create(provider={...})` raises `TypeError: Completions.create() got an unexpected keyword argument 'provider'` — this happens client-side, before any HTTP request. So the intended provider pin cannot function as written in the current code; the row is either produced by a different/older code path than what's on main today, or is running fully unpinned against OpenRouter's default fallback routing exactly as if no mitigation existed.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[configs/models/phi4/14b-reasoning-plus.yaml (api: openrouter, model slug ends `:free`, no provider/extra_body pin) — MathArena leaderboard entry "Phi-4-reasoning-plus"] Phi-4-reasoning-plus leaderboard row (human_readable_id "Phi-4-reasoning-plus", model microsoft/phi-4-reasoning-plus:free)
    → mechanism: M1 + M3: this config has zero provider pinning and explicitly targets OpenRouter's `:free` route, which routes across whichever community/rate-limited providers are currently offering the model for free — the segment of OpenRouter with the most run-to-run provider and quantization variance, and no cost signal to detect a downgrade.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[configs/models/qwen/qwen3_235b_a22b.yaml (api: openrouter, no provider/extra_body pin) — MathArena leaderboard entry "Qwen3-235B-A22B"] Qwen3-235B-A22B leaderboard row, original 2025-04-29 entry (human_readable_id "Qwen3-235B-A22B", model qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b)
    → mechanism: M1 + M3 directly, plus M8 (cross-provider comparison confound): this is the ONLY Qwen3 config in the repo without an `extra_body.provider` pin — the later configs qwen3_235b_a22b_2507.yaml, qwen3_vl_235b_a22b_thinking.yaml, qwen3_vl_235b_a22b_instruct.yaml, qwen3.5_397b.yaml and qwen3_30b_a3b.yaml all added the pin. The leaderboard ranks this unpinned row against its own pinned sibling releases and against proprietary single-served rows on nominally identical methodology, without controlling for the fact that only this row's inference backend/quantization is uncontrolled.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Structural risk across configs/models/{moonshot/k2.yaml, qwen/qwen3_235b_a22b.yaml, phi4/14b-reasoning-plus.yaml} — no ZDR/provider control visible in api_client.py's openrouter branch (L314-319)] Project-wide: MathArena's core "contamination-free" premise for the same OpenRouter-unpinned rows (Kimi K2, base Qwen3-235B-A22B, Phi-4-reasoning-plus:free)
    → mechanism: M5 (data-policy leakage): MathArena's entire value proposition is evaluating on newly-released, not-yet-public competition problems to avoid training contamination. For rows with no provider pin, the (still-unpublished) problem text is sent as a prompt through whichever third-party OpenRouter-listed provider happens to serve the request, subject to that provider's own data-retention/training policy rather than a policy MathArena controls — a latent risk to the contamination-free guarantee for exactly the rows already flagged above, independent of any single reported score.
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
Safeguards present: order+allow_fallbacks:False provider pinning (9 of 13 openrouter model configs), provenance_logging (full raw request/response capture via request_logger, which persists response.model_dump() -- openai-python's pydantic models use extra='allow' so OpenRouter's 'provider' field and generation id are preserved when present)
One-line fix: Add the same extra_body: {provider: {order: [<verified-provider>], allow_fallbacks: false}} pattern already used correctly on 9/13 openrouter configs to the 3 unpinned ones (kimi-k2, qwen3-235b-a22b, phi-4-reasoning-plus:free), and add quantizations + require_parameters:true to all 13 openrouter model configs.
Venue: ETH Zurich SRI Lab (Martin Vechev group)
Provider A/B rerun assessment: RECOMMENDED #1 — zero-config. configs/models/moonshot/k26.yaml already carries `extra_body: {provider: {allow_fallbacks: False, order: [moonshotai]}}`, splatted verbatim into the OpenAI SDK call at src/matharena/runner.py:293; six sibling configs use the same shape, so pinning is an established in-repo pattern and needs no source edit — copy the YAML and change the `order` list. Notable in its own right: the pinned `moonshotai` endpoint is int4 on OpenRouter, so MathArena's published Kimi-K2.6 leaderboard number is measured on an int4 backend while an fp8 endpoint (streamlake/fp8) of the same slug exists. A/B = moonshotai/int4 ($0.95/$4.00 per Mtok) vs streamlake/fp8 ($0.855/$3.60), both ~100% uptime. Amplification is frontier-reasoning: near-threshold competition math where one flipped token changes the final boxed integer, auto-graded so no judge confound. 30 problems x n=4 x 2 competitions x 3 conditions (strong / weak / strong-replicate for the noise floor) = 720 samples, ~7.2M output tokens, ~$42 total, ~1.9 h at the repo's own concurrent_requests=32.

Title: OSWorld (NeurIPS 2024 D&B) — mm_agents/{maestro,os_symphony,vlaa_gui} OpenRouter engine
Summary: OSWorld is the 369-task real-computer-use benchmark from the NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper. Its repo also hosts a growing library of third-party agent baselines under mm_agents/ (added well after the original paper); several of these (maestro, os_symphony, vlaa_gui) implement an OpenRouter engine as one of several pluggable LMM backends.
Importance/Impact: 3,019 GitHub stars; NeurIPS 2024 Datasets & Benchmarks paper; the standard real-computer-environment benchmark for multimodal/GUI agents (369 tasks across OS/web/desktop apps), widely adopted for computer-use agent evaluation.
What they use OpenRouter for: Agent rollout / action-generation backend, not just a judge: LMMEngineOpenRouter's output is parsed into the next pyautogui/tool action during live OSWorld task execution. In maestro's shipped default config (mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json), 10 of the agent's core cognitive roles (planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role, objective_alignment) default to "provider": "openrouter", "model_name": "openai/o3" — confirmed as the actual out-of-the-box config via the hardcoded load path in mm_agents/maestro/maestro/controller/config_manager.py:30-34.
Mistakes (null if safe): The maestro agent's shipped default config routes most of its decision-making roles through OpenRouter via the bare, undated slug "openai/o3" (M6) with data_collection left at OpenRouter's default "allow" (M5) despite full desktop screenshots being sent as images with every call, and the OpenRouter engine wrapper (duplicated near-verbatim in os_symphony and vlaa_gui) discards the API response after pulling out `.choices[0].message.content` and token usage, so the actually-served provider / generation id is never recorded anywhere (M4). Because this is the default action-generation policy for the maestro agent, any OSWorld benchmark score reported for maestro (or the analogous os_symphony/vlaa_gui agents) run with the shipped OpenRouter config cannot be tied to a specific backend snapshot, and could silently drift if OpenAI updates what "o3" resolves to behind that alias. Corrupting risks that would matter most for open-weight models — M1 unpinned quantization, M3 probabilistic provider load-balancing, M8 cross-provider confound — are not seriously in play here because every model actually routed through OpenRouter in this codebase (openai/*, x-ai/*, anthropic/*, google/*) is proprietary and effectively single-provider, so this does not corrupt the benchmark's task-success measurements the way it would for an open-weight policy model; the remaining risk is reproducibility/audit-trail and data-governance, not silently-wrong scores.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Agentic Lybic's (a.k.a. "maestro", mm_agents/maestro) published SOTA claim of 57.07% success rate on OSWorld (50-step, Table 1 of arXiv:2509.11067) is produced by routing all five core reasoning roles (Controller/Manager/Technician/Operator/Analyst) through OpenRouter via the bare, undated slug "openai/o3" — the paper explicitly cites this exact repo path as its reproducibility artifact, and the shipped default config leaves data_collection at OpenRouter's "allow" default while discarding all provider/response metadata.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Table 1 / abstract, arXiv:2509.11067 ("Agentic Lybic: Multi-Agent Execution System with Tiered Reasoning and Orchestration"); the paper states "Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/xlang-ai/OSWorld/tree/main/mm_agents/maestro to facilitate reproducibility" — i.e. this is literally the audited code path. The same run also underlies the 'maestro' entry on the OSWorld project's own hosted leaderboard (osworld-v1.xlang.ai).] "Agentic Lybic achieves a state-of-the-art 57.07% success rate in 50 steps" on OSWorld, beating CoAct-1 (56.39%), Agent S2.5 w/ o3 (54.21%), Jedi-7B w/ o3 (50.65%)
    → mechanism: Paper states 'For all core reasoning components (Controller, Manager, and the three Worker roles - Technician, Operator, and Analyst), we utilize OpenAI o3.' The shipped default config (mm_agents/maestro/tools/new_tools_config.json) routes exactly these roles (planner_role, supplement_role, dag_translator, operator_role, technician_role, analyst_role, worker_success_role, worker_stale_role, periodic_role, final_check_role — 10 of ~15 tool entries) through provider:"openrouter", model_name:"openai/o3". M6 (version drift): the slug is bare/undated, so the exact snapshot behind the reported number is unpinned and can silently change, contradicting the paper's stated reproducibility goal. M5 (data-policy leakage): LMMEngineOpenRouter (mm_agents/maestro/core/engine.py) never sets provider/data_collection, so OpenRouter's default 'allow' policy applies while full desktop screenshots are sent on every one of these calls during live task execution — undisclosed in the paper. M4 (no provenance logging): engine.generate() calls response.choices[0].message.content and calculate_tokens_and_cost(response,...) and returns only (content, total_tokens, cost) — the response object (including OpenRouter's actual-serving-provider field) is discarded, so neither the paper authors nor anyone attempting to reproduce 57.07% can verify which backend organization actually served the o3 calls behind it.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[mm_agents/os_symphony/core/engine.py and mm_agents/vlaa_gui/core/engine.py (ENGINE_REGISTRY entries "open_router"/"anthropic_bedrock"/etc.); contrast with OS-Symphony Table 1 (arXiv:2601.07779) and VLAA-GUI Table 2 (arXiv:2604.21375)] Downstream/class risk: any OSWorld success-rate result produced by running mm_agents/os_symphony or mm_agents/vlaa_gui with engine_type="open_router" (both ship a near-identical LMMEngineOpenRouter class copied from the same pattern) inherits the identical M4/M5/M6 exposure. This is distinct from — and should not be confused with — the two agents' own currently-published headline numbers: OS-Symphony's 65.84% (Table 1, arXiv:2601.07779, backbone "GPT-5") and VLAA-GUI's 77.45%/74.89% (arXiv:2604.21375, backbones Claude Opus 4.6/4.5), whose methods sections name only direct OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/ByteDance API access and never mention OpenRouter.
    → mechanism: Same M4/M5/M6 pattern as the maestro finding above would apply to anyone who selects the OpenRouter engine option for these agents, but as published the two papers' own headline claims do not appear to route through OpenRouter (VLAA-GUI's own README even states its default main reasoning model is direct "Anthropic Bedrock"), so this is a latent/inherited risk for future reproductions and leaderboard submissions of these two agents rather than a confirmed distortion of an already-reported number.
    → severity: low | depends-on-OpenRouter: low
One-line fix: In LMMEngineOpenRouter.generate() (engine.py in maestro/os_symphony/vlaa_gui), add extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}} and persist response.provider/response.id alongside each generation; pin "openai/o3" to a dated OpenRouter snapshot if one is exposed.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 8/10 (long-horizon agentic error compounding, structured-output adherence, multi-turn compounding). Blocker: HARD DISQUALIFIER: every OSWorld task execution requires a live virtual-machine desktop, not just an API call. maestro's actual entry points (mm_agents/maestro/osworld_run_maestro.py:173-178 and cli_app_maestro.py) construct `DesktopEnv(provider_name="vmware", path_to_vm="vmware_vm_data/Ubuntu0/Ubuntu0.vmx", ...)` — this requires VMware W...

Title: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin)
Summary: OpenHands is a widely-used (81k+ star) open-source platform for CodeAct-style generalist coding/browsing agents, evaluated across SWE-bench, WebArena, GAIA, and 15+ other benchmarks; its actual LLM-calling code now lives in a companion SDK repo, OpenHands/software-agent-sdk.
Importance/Impact: 81,136 GitHub stars, one of the most widely used open-source coding-agent platforms; evaluated across 15+ benchmarks including SWE-bench and WebArena; backbone for many downstream research SWE-bench/agent submissions (e.g. Agentless-Lite's OpenRouter-touting SWE-bench-lite entry lives in SWE-bench/experiments).
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is one of many pluggable "LLM Provider" backends an OpenHands user can select in Settings to run the agent's rollouts (coding tasks, SWE-bench-style, web browsing), implemented as a generic LiteLLM pass-through in openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py. Separately, OpenHands' own legacy AlgoTune benchmark adapter used OpenRouter directly to compare several open-weight models on a leaderboard.
Mistakes (null if safe): OpenHands ships zero default OpenRouter safeguards anywhere in its LLM class: no quantizations pin (M1), sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed/reasoning_effort) sent with no require_parameters (M2), no order/only/sort so routing stays load-balanced (M3), data_collection is never set so it defaults to "allow" (M5). The only lever is `litellm_extra_body`, an empty dict by default that a user must populate themselves with a `provider` block to get any of the above — nothing in the product docs (openrouter.mdx) shows this or warns about reproducibility, and the documented example model string is a bare undated slug (`openrouter/anthropic/claude-3.5-sonnet`, M6). Provenance logging (`log_completions`, which would capture the raw served response) defaults to False (M4) — so served-provider/generation-id is not recorded unless a user explicitly opts in. Concretely, legacy/algotune/adapter/utils.py:36-39 shows OpenHands itself comparing multiple open-weight models (GLM-4.5, Qwen3-Coder, GPT-OSS-120b) served over unpinned OpenRouter routes on a leaderboard with no visible provider constraint (M8). Docs give zero routing/reproducibility guidance (M10). Mitigating factor: OpenHands' own currently-maintained, canonical eval harness (github.com/OpenHands/benchmarks/.llm_config/example.json) routes its flagship SWE-bench-style numbers through a self-hosted `litellm_proxy/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514` proxy straight to Anthropic, NOT OpenRouter — so OpenHands' own headline paper/leaderboard claims are not directly corrupted. The risk this taxonomy flags falls mainly on (a) the historical AlgoTune leaderboard numbers, and (b) the many downstream research repos that reuse this exact zero-default OpenRouter integration for their own reported results.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: None of the paper's own reported numbers (Tables 3-6, arXiv:2407.16741) are produced via OpenRouter — every OpenHands-run result uses direct OpenAI/Anthropic API models (gpt-4o, gpt-4o-mini, gpt-3.5-turbo, gpt-4-turbo, claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620, claude-3-opus); the OpenRouter risk instead sits entirely downstream, in the class of community SWE-bench/GAIA/WebArena leaderboard submissions that run the OpenHands harness with 'LLM Provider = OpenRouter' for open-weight models, a fully-documented but zero-safeguard path.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[OpenHands evaluation harness (evaluation/benchmarks/{swe_bench,gaia,webarena} in github.com/OpenHands/OpenHands) run with the documented OpenRouter provider path (docs page openhands/usage/llms/openrouter.mdx); manifests externally as SWE-bench/GAIA leaderboard entries and vendor blog posts citing 'OpenHands' as scaffold, not inside arXiv:2407.16741 itself] Downstream SWE-bench Verified / GAIA / WebArena leaderboard and model-release-blog claims of the form 'OpenHands + <open-weight model> resolved X%' where the model was served through OpenRouter (e.g. DeepSeek-V3/R1, Qwen2.5/3-Coder, Kimi K2, GLM-4.5/4.6, Llama-3.1-405B) — not a number appearing in this paper's own Tables 3-6, but the class of results that inherit OpenHands' default OpenRouter configuration
    → mechanism: M1 (no quantizations pin — OpenRouter may silently serve an int4/int8 build of the open-weight model instead of full precision), M2 (litellm_extra_body defaults to {} so temperature/top_p/seed set by the eval harness are not enforced via require_parameters and can be silently dropped by whichever provider is picked), M3 (no order/only/sort, so the serving provider — and therefore latency, quantization, and quality — can change between runs or even mid-run), and M4 (no provenance logging, so a reported score can't be traced to which specific backend/quant actually produced it). Confirmed in code: the OpenRouter docs page (openhands/usage/llms/openrouter.mdx) gives zero guidance on any of this, and the SDK's LLM class (openhands-sdk/openhands/sdk/llm/llm.py) exposes only openrouter_site_url/openrouter_app_name (cosmetic HTTP-Referer/X-Title headers) plus the empty-by-default litellm_extra_body as the sole routing lever.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: Ship a safe default `provider` block (require_parameters:true, data_collection:"deny", pinned quantizations, order/sort set) whenever the OpenRouter backend is selected, and default `log_completions` on (or otherwise persist the served-provider/generation-id) so the actually-served weights are pinned and recorded instead of left to each downstream user/fork to configure themselves.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: DISQUALIFIED; amplification 0/10 (no strong amplification mechanism). Blocker: README states outright the code is moving: agent/SDK source now lives at github.com/OpenHands/software-agent-sdk, frontend at github.com/OpenHands/agent-canvas (README, 'Agent Canvas transition FAQ' section) — this repo is a product control-plane, not a research codebase.; Zero hits for 'algotune', 'evaluation', 'swebench'/'swe-bench' any...

Title: Prometheus 2 / BiGGen-Bench (KAIST AI)
Summary: BiGGen-Bench evaluates 103 LLMs (28 base + 61 chat, run locally via vllm, plus 14 proprietary API models) across 9 capabilities using 5 evaluator LMs (Prometheus-2 variants + Claude-3-Opus + GPT-4-1106 + GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09); prometheus-eval is the companion open-source judge-LM library.
Importance/Impact: 1102 GitHub stars. Prometheus/Prometheus 2 are widely cited open-source 'judge LM' papers from KAIST AI (Prometheus 1 has ~300+ citations), and BiGGen-Bench is their large fine-grained, per-capability judge benchmark used to evaluate dozens of LLMs.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (via litellm's `acompletion`/`completion`, wrapped in prometheus_eval/litellm.py's LiteLLM/AsyncLiteLLM classes) is used by BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py to generate candidate responses from API-only ("proprietary") LLMs for the benchmark — these responses are then scored by the 5 evaluator LMs and feed directly into the paper's Table 1, Figures 4-5 (performance-vs-size trends), and Table 4 (the paper's headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" analysis). The same wrapper is also written to support calling an external LLM (e.g. GPT-4) as judge in run_response_eval.py, though that specific call is currently commented out in favor of a MockLLM stub in the public repo.
Mistakes (null if safe): M4 (no provenance logging): confirmed — run_api_inference.py:70 only records the CLI `model_name` string as `response_model_name`; the actual served provider/backend/generation id for the OpenRouter-routed "proprietary LM" responses is never captured anywhere, so if OpenRouter ever silently switched backend for one of these models (e.g., OpenAI-direct vs Azure-hosted GPT-4), it would be undetectable and unreproducible. This threatens reproducibility of the candidate responses feeding Table 1/Figs 4-5/Table 4. M5 (data-policy leakage): confirmed — `data_collection` is never set to "deny" anywhere in the codebase, so prompts (including Safety-capability tasks about harm/secrets/moral dilemmas) default to OpenRouter's permissive data-collection routing. Doesn't corrupt results but is an unaddressed privacy/policy gap for a paper that explicitly discusses "Potential Risks" of its safety content without covering this angle. M6 (model version drift): the library's own documented usage example (libs/prometheus-eval/README.md:70) calls an undated `gpt-4-turbo` alias, and `run_api_inference.py --model_name` accepts any free-form string with no enforcement of dated slugs — this threatens exact reproducibility of any candidate-model responses generated this way, since OpenAI/other providers periodically repoint undated aliases to new checkpoints. M10 (no reporting): the BiGGen-Bench paper's Limitations section covers several reproducibility threats (stochastic generation, verbosity bias, self-enhancement bias) at length but is silent on the API-routing/inference-stack risk for the subset of the 103 evaluated models obtained via OpenRouter, despite this feeding the paper's own headline "gap between open-source and proprietary LMs" comparison (Table 4).
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Table 4/Table 11's headline "open-source vs. proprietary LM" performance gap (Hedges's g = 0.51 average, ranging 0.36–0.84 by capability) rests on comparing proprietary-model responses generated via litellm/OpenRouter against open-weight responses run locally under controlled vLLM decoding — an apples-to-oranges comparison if OpenRouter silently altered quantization, provider, or sampling params on the proprietary side.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[Table 11 (Appendix D.3) / Table 4 (main text, Section 4)] "Performance Gap between Open-source Chat and Proprietary LMs Across Capabilities" — Hedges's g effect sizes: Average 0.51, Instruction Following 0.38, Grounding 0.49, Planning 0.58, Reasoning 0.65, Refinement 0.46, Safety 0.36, Theory of Mind 0.59, Tool Usage 0.58, Multilingual 0.84 (condensed version appears as main-text Table 4)
    → mechanism: M8 (cross-provider comparison confound) + M2 (silent param dropping): the proprietary-side candidate responses feeding this table are generated by BiGGen-Bench/run_api_inference.py via prometheus_eval's AsyncLiteLLM wrapper, and the repo README states verbatim 'we mainly used OpenAI API and OpenRouter' for this exact script — while the open-source side is run locally via vLLM at fixed precision. Independently confirmed by reading prometheus_eval/litellm.py: AsyncLiteLLM's async completion path (`_get_completion_text_async`) accepts `**kwargs` but never forwards it to litellm's `acompletion()` call — only model/api_base/messages/timeout are passed — so the temperature=1.0/top_p=0.9/max_tokens=2048 the paper intends for API models is silently dropped client-side before OpenRouter even sees the request, meaning the two arms of this comparison were never run under matched, controlled sampling conditions. Any OpenRouter-side quantization or provider switching on top of that directly inflates or deflates the proprietary arm's scores, biasing every effect size in this table.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Table 6 (main text, Section 4)] "Top 1 to 5 LMs on each capability scored by GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09" — leaderboard rankings, e.g. Average: GPT-4-1106 > GPT-4-Turbo-0125 > GPT-4o-2024-05-13 > GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 > Claude-3-Opus; Reasoning top-5 includes Gemini-Pro-1.5; Safety top-2: Claude-3-Opus, GPT-4-1106
    → mechanism: M8 + M3 (probabilistic routing / run-to-run provider switching): nearly every top-5 slot in most capability columns is filled by closely-clustered proprietary models (GPT-4 variants, Claude-3-Opus/Sonnet, Gemini-Pro-1.5) whose candidate responses were generated through the same OpenRouter/OpenAI-API path as Finding 1. Litellm/OpenRouter can route nominally-identical model calls to different backend providers or quantization levels between runs, and fine-grained ordinal rankings among tightly-scored proprietary models are exactly the kind of claim such routing noise can flip (e.g. which GPT-4 snapshot ranks #1 overall).
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[Table 3 (Section 5.1) plus Figures 4-5 and Tables 1-2 (Section 4), which all use GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 as evaluator LM] Every score produced by the three proprietary evaluator LMs — Table 3 Pearson correlations with human judgment (Claude-3-Opus 0.624, GPT-4-1106 0.641, GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 0.647, Majority Voting 0.627) and, since GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 is used as the sole evaluator for the paper's primary scaling analyses, essentially all of Figure 4, Figure 5, Table 1, Table 2, and the GLM results in Tables 8-10
    → mechanism: M9 (judge on unconstrained route): the exact call site is BiGGen-Bench/run_response_eval.py lines 124-125 (`model = MockLLM(mode="absolute"); # model = AsyncLiteLLM(eval_model_name, batch_size=100, requests_per_minute=100)`) — confirmed to match the cited primary OpenRouter call site; the released repo mocks this out publicly but it is the documented replacement used to actually produce the paper's judgments. Score extraction relies on parsing a free-text '[RESULT] N' suffix with no structured-output constraint, so any provider variation in instruction-following behavior shifts scores. Appendix G.1 explicitly claims 'we use hyperparameters of temperature 0.0 ... to ensure reproducibility for both the evaluator language model and the language model being evaluated,' but AsyncLiteLLM's async path never forwards sampling kwargs to acompletion() at all (same bug as Finding 1), so this reproducibility claim was likely not actually enforced for the API-routed judges — leaving the judge calls exposed to whatever decoding defaults and provider/quantization OpenRouter (or the underlying API) chose. Because GPT-4-Turbo-2024-04-09 alone is the evaluator behind nearly every headline figure/table in Sections 4-5, this is the single largest-blast-radius risk, but confidence it specifically routed through OpenRouter (vs. direct OpenAI/Anthropic keys) is only medium: the README's explicit OpenRouter statement is scoped to the response-generation script, not this evaluation script, and the call is mocked/commented out in the public code so the exact production configuration cannot be directly verified (this itself is the M4 no-provenance-logging gap already identified for the project).
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: medium
One-line fix: In prometheus_eval/litellm.py, forward `**kwargs` into the `acompletion()`/`completion()` calls and set `extra_body={"provider": {"data_collection": "deny", "require_parameters": True}}` plus store `response.get("provider")` and `response.id` alongside each output in run_api_inference.py's result dict; also require dated model slugs for any `--model_name` passed to run_api_inference.py.

Title: openbench (Groq)
Summary: openbench is Groq's official, provider-agnostic Inspect-AI-based evaluation harness (95+ benchmarks including GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena) that ships OpenRouter as a first-class model backend via a dedicated `OpenRouterAPI` wrapper.
Importance/Impact: Authored by Groq (major AI inference-hardware company); 784 stars; positions itself explicitly as 'provider-agnostic, open-source evaluation infrastructure for language models' with OpenRouter as a named first-class provider (`openrouter/model-name` prefix, OPENROUTER_API_KEY) in its README provider table.
What they use OpenRouter for: Dedicated OpenRouter provider wrapper (OpenRouterAPI, subclassing Inspect AI's OpenAICompatibleAPI) used as one of 30+ selectable backends to run chat-completion calls for openbench's 95+ evaluation suites (GPQA Diamond, AIME 2023-2025, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, medical/legal/reasoning QA sets, etc.), producing the accuracy scores that are the tool's entire output/deliverable.
Mistakes (null if safe): The repo is best understood as infrastructure whose safety depends entirely on how a downstream user invokes it — but its shipped defaults and its own documented examples for the highest-stakes evals never demonstrate a safe invocation, threatening any benchmark score (GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU, etc.) a user reports after running `bench eval <task> --model openrouter/<open-weight-model>` without manually adding `-M` flags. M1 (unpinned quantization): default `provider` payload omits `quantizations`, so OpenRouter can silently serve int4/int8 weights, directly threatening any accuracy number from a reasoning/math eval, which is exactly the class of benchmark most sensitive to precision loss. M2 (silent parameter dropping): evals like gpqa_diamond.py set `temperature=0.5` and the CLI exposes `--seed`, but `require_parameters` is never set by default, so a provider that doesn't honor these params drops them without error, silently changing the sampling distribution behind a reported score. M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the base OpenRouterAPI ships no `order`/`only`/`sort` by default, so repeated runs of the same eval load-balance across different backend providers/engines — this is only mitigated in ~10 non-critical eval docstrings via `-M only=groq`, and never demonstrated for GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` defaults to OpenRouter's server-side "allow", meaning eval prompts (which may include held-out benchmark items) can be logged/trained on by third-party providers unless a user manually passes `-M data_collection=deny`. M7 (seed→determinism): `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` is documented as producing "deterministic generation" with no caveat that provider routing/backend choice breaks that guarantee, and the same param is subject to M2's silent-drop risk. Mitigating factor working against a harsher verdict: unlike most repos, ALL the necessary safeguards (quantizations, require_parameters, order/only/sort, data_collection) are fully implemented and exposed as first-class configurable `-M` flags — this is well above the median repo's OpenRouter hygiene — and the README's "Reproducibility Statement" shows the maintainers are aware quantization variance is a real issue, even though they don't act on that awareness by defaulting to safe settings or by logging provenance.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: Any benchmark score (MMLU, SimpleQA, GPQA Diamond, AIME, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, etc.) that a downstream user reports from openbench's OWN documented OpenRouter invocations is at risk, because the OpenRouterAPI wrapper (src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py L57-115) leaves quantization, parameter-enforcement, and data-collection controls unset (None) unless the user manually adds `-M` flags — and openbench's own docs examples never demonstrate the fully-safe combination.
Possibly-impacted findings (specific fig:
[docs/providers.mdx, section 'OpenRouter' (code block after the parameter table)] The official OpenRouter usage example in openbench's own docs: `bench eval mmlu --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b --max-connections 100 -M only=groq,together -M sort=price` (docs/providers.mdx, 'OpenRouter Configuration Options' section)
    → mechanism: M1: no `-M quantizations=` is set, so OpenRouter can silently serve gpt-oss-120b (an open-weight MoE model) at any quantization level from either backend down to int4. M3: `only=groq,together` still leaves probabilistic selection between two backends/engines on every request — the same command can silently switch which provider (and therefore which quantization/engine) answered each question, and re-running it later is not guaranteed to reproduce the same routing. M3/cost-bias: `sort=price` explicitly asks OpenRouter to prefer the cheapest available route, which correlates with more aggressively quantized/degraded serving. This is the flagship copy-pasteable example for OpenRouter in the repo's own documentation, so any MMLU (or other benchmark, since only the task name changes) score produced by following it verbatim inherits all three issues.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[docs/providers.mdx, `<Tip>` box under 'General Model Configuration'] The docs' secondary OpenRouter 'Tip' example: `bench eval simpleqa --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b -M only=groq` (docs/providers.mdx, Tip callout)
    → mechanism: M1: still no `quantizations=` pin — OpenRouter's Groq-backed listing for gpt-oss-120b can still change quantization/serving parameters over time without the caller knowing (M6/M1 overlap). M2: `require_parameters` is left unset (defaults to None/False-equivalent in the wrapper), so if the OpenRouter route silently drops sampling params (temperature/top_p/seed/reasoning_effort) that SimpleQA's answer-extraction/grading logic depends on, the call succeeds with silently altered behavior rather than erroring. Restricting to `only=groq` does meaningfully reduce M3 cross-provider switching risk relative to Finding 1, hence lower severity here.
    → severity: medium | depends-on-OpenRouter: high

[src/openbench/model/_providers/openrouter.py L57-115 (constructor defaults: `only=None, order=None, allow_fallbacks=None, ignore=None, sort=None, max_price=None, quantizations=None, require_parameters=None, data_collection=None`) combined with README.md 'Speedrun' section and the CLI default-options table (`--seed` default `None`, `--temperature 0.6`)] The general class of downstream results across ALL 95+ openbench suites (explicitly including GPQA Diamond, AIME 2023-2025, MATH, MathVista, MathArena, MMLU) reported by any user who follows openbench's own 'Speedrun' invocation pattern (`bench eval <task> --model openrouter/<open-weight-model> --limit N`) but substitutes `openrouter/` for the README's `groq/` example, adding zero `-M` flags — the naive default path the README's own quickstart pattern invites
    → mechanism: With every provider_params key left None, `self._extra_body` is empty and NO provider-routing constraints are injected into the request at all — full unconstrained OpenRouter default routing applies. M1: quantization completely unpinned for whichever open-weight model is named. M2: no `require_parameters=True`, so any sampling/format param the randomly-selected backend doesn't support is silently dropped rather than causing an error. M3: with `order`/`only`/`sort` all unset, OpenRouter load-balances across all eligible providers per-request, so a single benchmark run can mix quantizations/engines across samples within the same eval, and the run is not reproducible run-to-run. M5: `data_collection` unset means OpenRouter's default data-retention policy applies to eval prompts (including competition-math questions from AIME/MathArena, which are contamination-sensitive) unless the user separately knows to pass `-M data_collection=deny`. M7: openbench exposes a documented `--seed`/`BENCH_SEED` flag implying deterministic generation is achievable, but seed pinning does not confer determinism across OpenRouter's multi-provider/multi-engine routing — a user who sets `--seed` for reproducibility on an `openrouter/` model gets a false sense of reproducibility.
    → severity: high | depends-on-OpenRouter: high
Safeguards present: only/order/ignore/sort/max_price (opt-in via -M flags), quantizations (opt-in via -M flags), require_parameters (opt-in via -M flags), data_collection (opt-in via -M flags), raw response captured in Inspect AI transcript via completion.model_dump() (inherited from inspect_ai, not surfaced by openbench itself), README Reproducibility Statement discloses quantization-driven score variance
One-line fix: Default the OpenRouterAPI wrapper to a safe `provider` payload (quantizations allow-list, require_parameters=True whenever sampling/seed params are set, data_collection="deny") and surface the served `provider`/generation-id from OpenRouter's response into openbench's per-run results/log output, then demonstrate that safe pattern in the GPQA/AIME/MATH/MathArena eval docstrings instead of leaving pinning entirely opt-in via undocumented-by-example `-M` flags.
Provider A/B rerun assessment: RECOMMENDED #2 — zero-config, and the cheapest decisive test we found. `bench eval gpt_oss_aime25 --model openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b -M provider='{...}'` reaches the wire: openbench's `-M` is yaml.safe_load-parsed (_cli/utils.py:39-59) and forwarded as model_args into inspect_ai's eval(), whose OpenRouterAPI collects a `provider` dict kwarg and emits it as extra_body.provider (verified in inspect_ai/model/_providers/openrouter.py, __init__ + completion_params). NOTE: the `-M only=Cerebras` form is wrong — inspect wants the whole dict under `provider`. Best precision gap available anywhere in the survey: cerebras/fp16 ($0.35/$0.75, 100% uptime, logprobs) vs wandb/fp4 ($0.04/$0.14, 95% uptime, logprobs) — fp16 vs fp4 on the same slug, and both expose logprobs so the divergence can also be read out distributionally. 30 AIME-2025 questions x epochs=8 (hardcoded) = 240 completions per arm; 3 conditions = 720 completions, ~5.8M output tokens, ~$3.20 total, well under 1 h.

Title: tau2-bench (Sierra AI)
Summary: tau2-bench is Sierra AI's widely-cited agentic tool-use/user-simulation benchmark (successor to tau-bench). It evaluates agent LLMs across domains (retail, airline, telecom, banking_knowledge) with a public leaderboard at taubench.com.
Importance/Impact: 1,601 GitHub stars; tau-bench/tau2-bench (Sierra AI) is one of the most widely cited tool-agent-user interaction benchmarks (original tau-bench arXiv:2406.12045), used as a standard evaluation for function-calling/agentic LLMs industry-wide.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter is used exclusively as the API backend for one component: dense-embedding retrieval in the `banking_knowledge` domain's RAG tool (`KB_search_dense`/`KB_search`). `OpenRouterEmbedder` (src/tau2/knowledge/embedders/openrouter_embedder.py) wraps the OpenAI SDK pointed at `base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"` and calls `.embeddings.create()` for Qwen3-Embedding-4B/8B (open-weight) and gemini-embedding-001. This is NOT the default retrieval config (default `alltools` uses OpenAI embeddings directly), but it is a first-class, documented, leaderboard-eligible option: `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` and `src/tau2/knowledge/README.md` both give runnable examples (`tau2 run --domain banking_knowledge --retrieval-config alltools-qwen --agent-llm gpt-4.1 --user-llm gpt-4.1 --num-trials 4`) that community members can submit to the public taubench.com leaderboard. The primary agent/user/judge LLM calls go through litellm directly to proprietary provider APIs (OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini), not through OpenRouter, by default — confirmed via `src/tau2/config.py` (DEFAULT_LLM_AGENT="gpt-4.1-2025-04-14", DEFAULT_LLM_EVAL_USER_SIMULATOR="claude-opus-4-5"). A test file (`tests/test_review_model.py`) shows an `"openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4"` litellm-prefixed string is accepted for the optional --review-model flag, but this is a mocked unit test of a generic litellm passthrough, not a built OpenRouter integration, and even if used it's a proprietary model (low routing risk).
Mistakes (null if safe): M1 (unpinned quantization): OpenRouterEmbedder.SUPPORTED_MODELS includes qwen3-embedding-4b/8b, open-weight models routed through OpenRouter's default load-balanced multi-provider serving with no `provider.quantizations` filter anywhere in the codebase — confirmed via full-repo grep for `quantizations` (zero hits). M3 (probabilistic provider routing): the `.embeddings.create()` call at openrouter_embedder.py:146 passes only `input`, `model`, `encoding_format` — no `extra_body={'provider': {...}}`, no order/only/sort anywhere (zero `extra_body` hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default inverse-price load balancing picks whichever backend serves Qwen3-Embedding on each call/run. M4 (no provenance logging): neither the embedder nor `embeddings_cache.py` (EmbeddingsCache keys purely on embedder_type/model/query_instruction hash) ever captures the response's `provider` field or a generation id, so which backend actually served a given embedding is unrecoverable after the fact. M5 (data-policy leakage): `data_collection` is never set to "deny" (zero hits repo-wide), so OpenRouter's default "allow" may route KB document/query text to providers that train on submitted data. All four apply to any run using the `qwen_embeddings*` or `alltools-qwen` retrieval-config family in the `banking_knowledge` domain — this is the retrieval backend for the agent's KB_search_dense tool, so embedding-quality drift from unpinned quantization/provider selection can shift which documents the agent retrieves and therefore its task pass/fail outcome. Because `docs/leaderboard-submission.md` explicitly documents `alltools-qwen` as a runnable leaderboard-submission config, any pass^k score submitted to the public taubench.com leaderboard using that config is a reported number whose reproducibility this gap threatens. The blast radius is bounded: this is an opt-in, non-default config touching only the retrieval component of one domain (banking_knowledge), not the primary agent/user/judge LLM calls (which go via litellm directly to proprietary APIs by default), and the original arXiv paper (June 2025) predates this OpenRouter/Qwen retrieval feature so its headline numbers are not implicated.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: The arXiv paper (2506.07982, telecom/retail/airline only) reports zero OpenRouter-touched numbers, but on the live taubench.com leaderboard the GPT-5.2 (reasoning_effort=none) banking_knowledge row — pass^1=12.63, pass^2=7.22, pass^3=5.15, pass^4=4.12 — was produced with retrieval_config="qwen_embeddings", which routes every KB_search dense-retrieval call through OpenRouterEmbedder to qwen3-embedding-8b with no quantization pin and no provider filter.

[taubench.com leaderboard, banking_knowledge domain -> web/leaderboard/public/submissions/gpt-5-2-none_sierra_2026-02-26/submission.json in sierra-research/tau2-bench] GPT-5.2 (reasoning_effort: none) banking_knowledge leaderboard scores: pass^1=12.63, pass^2=7.22, pass^3=5.15, pass^4=4.12 (submission gpt-5-2-none_sierra_2026-02-26/submission.json, notes: "Banking domain evaluated with retrieval_config: qwen_embeddings")
    → mechanism: M1+M3: this row's retrieval_config is 'qwen_embeddings', which per src/tau2/knowledge/embeddings_cache.py resolves to backend='openrouter', model='qwen3-embedding-8b'. Every KB_search dense-retrieval embedding call for every task in this run went through OpenRouterEmbedder.embeddings.create() at openrouter_embedder.py:146, which passes only input/model/encoding_format -- no extra_body={'provider':...} filter and no provider.quantizations pin anywhere in the repo (confirmed zero grep hits for 'quantizations'). Qwen3-Embedding-8B is open-weight and load-balanced across OpenRouter providers by default, so embeddings for this eval run could silently have come from an int4/int8-quantized replica, and a re-run could land on a different provider entirely -- both change the embedding vectors used for cosine retrieval in KB_search_dense, which directly gates whether the agent finds the correct banking KB passage and therefore whether each task scores pass/fail.     → mechanism: M1+M3+M4: the benchmark's own docs actively steer submitters toward the OR-routed qwen configs as one of two standard embedding options (the other being OpenAI). Title: R1 CoT Illegibility Revisited (nostalgebraist, fork of Jozdien/cot_legibility)
Summary: A LessWrong post/repo re-running DeepSeek-R1 GPQA CoT-legibility/correctness evaluations via OpenRouter, explicitly to test whether the original paper's Targon-served results generalize to other providers (Novita, Azure) — the post's core finding IS that OpenRouter provider choice changes R1's measured illegibility (2.30 mean on Novita vs 4.30 on Targon).
Importance/Impact: Author nostalgebraist is one of the most well-known and widely-read independent alignment/interpretability writers on LessWrong (creator of logit lens, long-running influential essayist). This post investigates DeepSeek-R1 chain-of-thought illegibility/faithfulness — a core AI-safety topic — and required carefully selecting OpenRouter's provider-routing to reproduce specific R1 behavior, illustrating OpenRouter's provider-level nuances affecting research reproducibility.
What they use OpenRouter for: OpenRouter (openai-sdk-compatible client, base_url=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1) is used to run inference on the open-weight model under study, DeepSeek-R1 (model_id "deepseek/deepseek-r1"), for the GPQA CoT-legibility and correctness evaluation whose statistics (mean illegibility, %>5, correctness %) are reported directly in the LessWrong post. Grading/judging (gpt-4o, claude-3-7-sonnet-latest) is done via direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, NOT via OpenRouter.
Headline possibly-impacted claim: None — the headline Novita-vs-Targon comparison (illegibility 2.28 vs 4.30; %>=5 illegible 0.5% vs 34.9%; correctness 40.5% vs 36.6%) is not distorted by OpenRouter routing, because the repo pins provider with allow_fallbacks:false and logs per-response provider confirmation showing 100% Novita on every one of the 198 sampled responses.
Why nothing impacted:
I verified the actual code and run artifacts, not just the post text, and every plausible mistake vector is closed off for the results this post reports:

1. M3 (probabilistic routing) / M11 (silent backend mixing) — closed. `src/inference/providers.py` (OpenRouterProvider.generate) builds `extra_body["provider"] = {"only": [provider_config], "allow_fallbacks": False}` whenever `openrouter_provider` is set in the model config. The config actually used for the headline numbers, `config/2026_04_19__r1__all__novita_only.yaml` (and the matching `runs/20260419_100716_R1_gpqa/config.yaml`, `runs/20260419_103551_R1_gpqa/config.yaml`), sets `openrouter_provider: [novita]`. I pulled the raw `inference.json` for the 200-question run and checked every record's logged `metadata.openrouter_provider` and `metadata.provider_model`: all 198 non-empty responses show `"openrouter_provider": "Novita"`, `"provider_model": "deepseek/deepseek-r1"` — no run-to-run provider drift, no mixing.

2. M4 (no provenance logging) — closed. The provider code explicitly captures `last_chunk.provider` and `last_chunk.model` off the OpenRouter streaming response into every stored record (`result["openrouter_provider"]`, `result["provider_model"]`), so each of the reported data points carries its own served-provider confirmation — this is exactly the provenance discipline the mistake category is about, already implemented.

3. M1 (unpinned quantization) — largely mitigated for the load-bearing comparison. The post itself states both Targon and Novita were confirmed running fp8, i.e., the author checked for a quantization confound between the two conditions being compared rather than assuming it away. Residual risk is low-to-negligible: pinning to a single named provider (Novita) rather than a `provider/quant` string doesn't cryptographically guarantee quantization never changes server-side, but this is a minor residual, not a finding that plausibly reverses or meaningfully shifts the reported gap (0.5% vs 34.9% illegible-≥5, 2.28 vs 4.30 mean illegibility) — the effect size is far too large to be a quantization artifact.

4. M9 (judge on unconstrained route) — not applicable. The grader model for the reported runs is `gpt-4o` (`evaluation.grader_model: gpt-4o` in the run configs), and `src/utils/models.py`'s `MODEL_REGISTRY` maps `"gpt-4o"` to `{"provider": "openai", ...}`, which `get_provider()` routes to `DirectAPIProvider("openai")` — a direct `OPENAI_API_KEY` OpenAI SDK call, never touching `openrouter.ai`. So the illegibility/correctness judge scores that produce the headline numbers are not OpenRouter-routed at all.

5. M8 (cross-provider comparison confound) — not applicable as a "mistake": the Targon-vs-Novita comparison is the intentional object of study, not an accidental confound smuggled into an unrelated result. The Targon baseline numbers are pulled from the original paper's archived rollouts (via `scripts/convert_archived_rollouts.py`, feeding `runs/2025101*_R1_gpqa` analysis-only runs with no `inference` stage), not re-generated by this repo's own uncontrolled OpenRouter calls.

6. M2/M6/M7 (param dropping, version drift, seed) — no evidence of impact: `temperature: 1.0` and `include_reasoning: true` are explicitly set and passed through every call; no `max_tokens` cap is set for the R1 runs (avoiding the truncation problem the post itself flags for Azure, which is excluded from the reported comparison for exactly that reason).

Given the explicit provider pin with `allow_fallbacks: False`, the empirically-verified 100% single-provider provenance log across every sampled response, and a judge model that bypasses OpenRouter entirely, none of the reported findings (the illegibility means, the ≥5-threshold percentages, or the correctness-rate breakdown) plausibly route through an OpenRouter mistake pathway. This is consistent with the pre-assigned SAFE verdict.</no_impact_reason>
Safeguards present: order/only provider pinning (extra_body['provider']={'only':['novita'],'allow_fallbacks':False}), provenance logging of served provider and provider_model per-call, verified empirically in output data, grader/judge runs on direct OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, not OpenRouter, so no judge route to corrupt, provider-routing effect made the explicit subject of the study and disclosed at length in the post
Provider A/B rerun assessment: HIGHEST-VALUE EVIDENCE, AND IT IS ALREADY FREE. This is the repo our survey marked SAFE, and its committed runs/ directory already contains the natural experiment: same slug deepseek/deepseek-r1, same GPQA-diamond pipeline, provenance logged per response (src/inference/providers.py:104). Unpinned runs served mostly by Targon score mean illegibility 4.31 +/- 2.13 (n=295) at 36.6% accuracy; provider-pinned Novita runs score 2.31 +/- 0.75 (n=198) at 43.9% — an ~88% swing in the headline metric under one model name. One unpinned run drifted mid-experiment (only 169/300 responses from Targon) and landed at 31.4% accuracy, a 12.5-point spread against a pinned run. Caveat: those runs are 6 months apart, so checkpoint drift is confounded with provider — which is precisely the argument for the paired same-day rerun. Rerunning is zero-config (`openrouter_provider` is a first-class YAML field; the author pins in endpoint-tag form e.g. `targon/fp8`, `deepinfra/bf16`, confirming provider.only accepts slug/quant). Weakness: deepseek/deepseek-r1 now has only 2 live endpoints — azure (ctx 163840, $1.485/$5.94) vs novita/fp8 (ctx 64000, $0.70/$2.50) — a modest, partly-unknown quality gap. ~$15 and ~1 h if rerun; $0 to cite the existing numbers.
